How would you improve the architecture of this codebase, especially if the goal was to squeeze even more performance out of it, noting that the most important items coming up are:

- adding durability to the logs and the corresponding "replay"
- improving performance likely via zero copy behaviour across the board (inc to network and disk stacks)

correctness, performance, latency - no compromise

    tool result
    1	use crate::outbox::Outbox;
2	use crate::ring::{channel, Consumer, Producer, RingError};
3	use crate::sequencer::run_core;
4	use crate::telemetry::{now_mono_ns, Telemetry, TelemetryConfig};
5	use crate::wire::{
6	    self, decode_hello, encode_goaway, encode_hello_ack, encode_transport_frame, FrameKind,
7	    HelloAck, TransportFrame, SCHEMA_VERSION,
8	};
9	use std::io::{self, ErrorKind, Read, Write};
10	use std::net::{Shutdown, TcpListener, TcpStream};
11	use std::path::{Path, PathBuf};
12	use std::sync::atomic::{AtomicBool, Ordering};
13	use std::sync::Arc;
14	use std::thread;
15	use std::time::{Duration, Instant};
16	
17	const SERVER_MAX_FRAME_LENGTH: u32 = 1_048_576;
18	const DEFAULT_HEARTBEAT_MS: u32 = 1000;
19	const RING_CAPACITY: usize = 1024;
20	
21	#[derive(Clone)]
22	pub struct RuntimeConfig {
23	    pub run_dir: PathBuf,
24	    pub ordp_endpoint: String,
25	    pub outbox_path: PathBuf,
26	    pub telemetry_enabled: bool,
27	}
28	
29	pub fn run_from_env() -> io::Result<()> {
30	    let mut run_dir = None;
31	    let mut telemetry = std::env::var("SPINE_TELEMETRY").is_ok_and(|v| v == "1");
32	    for arg in std::env::args().skip(1) {
33	        if arg == "--telemetry" {
34	            telemetry = true;
35	        } else if run_dir.is_none() {
36	            run_dir = Some(PathBuf::from(arg));
37	        }
38	    }
39	    let run_dir = run_dir
40	        .or_else(|| std::env::var_os("SPINE_RUN_DIR").map(PathBuf::from))
41	        .ok_or_else(|| io::Error::new(ErrorKind::InvalidInput, "missing run dir"))?;
42	    let config = load_runtime_config(&run_dir, telemetry)?;
43	    run(config)
44	}
45	
46	pub fn load_runtime_config(run_dir: &Path, telemetry_enabled: bool) -> io::Result<RuntimeConfig> {
47	    let ordp_endpoint = std::env::var("SPINE_ORDP_ENDPOINT").map_err(|_| {
48	        io::Error::new(
49	            ErrorKind::InvalidInput,
50	            "missing SPINE_ORDP_ENDPOINT environment variable",
51	        )
52	    })?;
53	    let outbox_path = std::env::var("SPINE_OBOX_PATH").map_err(|_| {
54	        io::Error::new(
55	            ErrorKind::InvalidInput,
56	            "missing SPINE_OBOX_PATH environment variable",
57	        )
58	    })?;
59	    Ok(RuntimeConfig {
60	        run_dir: run_dir.to_path_buf(),
61	        ordp_endpoint,
62	        outbox_path: PathBuf::from(outbox_path),
63	        telemetry_enabled,
64	    })
65	}
66	
67	pub fn run(config: RuntimeConfig) -> io::Result<()> {
68	    std::fs::create_dir_all(&config.run_dir)?;
69	    let listener = TcpListener::bind(&config.ordp_endpoint)?;
70	
71	    let outbox = Outbox::create(&config.outbox_path)?;
72	    publish_descriptor(&config)?;
73	
74	    let (mut stream, _) = listener.accept()?;
75	    let negotiated = handshake(&mut stream)?;
76	
77	    let (ingress_producer, ingress_consumer) = channel(RING_CAPACITY);
78	    let (ack_producer, ack_consumer) = channel(RING_CAPACITY);
79	    let input_drained = Arc::new(AtomicBool::new(false));
80	    let writer_done = Arc::new(AtomicBool::new(false));
81	
82	    let reader_stream = stream.try_clone()?;
83	    let writer_stream = stream;
84	
85	    let reader = {
86	        let input_drained = Arc::clone(&input_drained);
87	        thread::spawn(move || {
88	            reader_loop(
89	                reader_stream,
90	                ingress_producer,
91	                input_drained,
92	                negotiated.max_frame_length,
93	            )
94	        })
95	    };
96	    let core = {
97	        let input_drained = Arc::clone(&input_drained);
98	        let writer_done = Arc::clone(&writer_done);
99	        let telemetry = Telemetry::new(TelemetryConfig {
100	            enabled: config.telemetry_enabled,
101	            metrics_path: config.run_dir.join("metrics.json"),
102	            latency_path: config.run_dir.join("publish-ts.bin"),
103	        });
104	        thread::spawn(move || {
105	            run_core(
106	                ingress_consumer,
107	                ack_producer,
108	                input_drained,
109	                writer_done,
110	                outbox,
111	                telemetry,
112	            )
113	        })
114	    };
115	    let writer = {
116	        let writer_done = Arc::clone(&writer_done);
117	        thread::spawn(move || {
118	            writer_loop(
119	                writer_stream,
120	                ack_consumer,
121	                writer_done,
122	                negotiated.heartbeat_interval_ms,
123	            )
124	        })
125	    };
126	
127	    join_io(reader, "reader")?;
128	    join_io(core, "core")?;
129	    join_io(writer, "writer")?;
130	    Ok(())
131	}
132	
133	#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
134	struct Negotiated {
135	    max_frame_length: u32,
136	    heartbeat_interval_ms: u32,
137	}
138	
139	fn handshake(stream: &mut TcpStream) -> io::Result<Negotiated> {
140	    let frame = read_frame(stream, SERVER_MAX_FRAME_LENGTH)?;
141	    if frame.kind != FrameKind::Hello {
142	        reject_handshake(stream)?;
143	        return Err(io::Error::new(ErrorKind::InvalidData, "expected HELLO"));
144	    }
145	    let hello = match decode_hello(&frame.payload) {
146	        Ok(v) => v,
147	        Err(_) => {
148	            reject_handshake(stream)?;
149	            return Err(io::Error::new(ErrorKind::InvalidData, "bad HELLO"));
150	        }
151	    };
152	    if hello.min_schema_version > SCHEMA_VERSION || hello.max_schema_version < SCHEMA_VERSION {
153	        reject_handshake(stream)?;
154	        return Err(io::Error::new(ErrorKind::InvalidData, "unsupported schema"));
155	    }
156	    let negotiated = Negotiated {
157	        max_frame_length: hello.max_frame_length.min(SERVER_MAX_FRAME_LENGTH),
158	        heartbeat_interval_ms: if hello.heartbeat_interval_ms == 0 {
159	            DEFAULT_HEARTBEAT_MS
160	        } else {
161	            hello.heartbeat_interval_ms
162	        },
163	    };
164	    let mut payload = Vec::new();
165	    encode_hello_ack(
166	        HelloAck {
167	            schema_version: SCHEMA_VERSION,
168	            max_frame_length: negotiated.max_frame_length,
169	            heartbeat_interval_ms: negotiated.heartbeat_interval_ms,
170	            result: 0,
171	        },
172	        &mut payload,
173	    );
174	    let mut frame = Vec::new();
175	    encode_transport_frame(FrameKind::HelloAck, &payload, &mut frame);
176	    stream.write_all(&frame)?;
177	    stream.flush()?;
178	    Ok(negotiated)
179	}
180	
181	fn reject_handshake(stream: &mut TcpStream) -> io::Result<()> {
182	    let mut payload = Vec::new();
183	    encode_hello_ack(
184	        HelloAck {
185	            schema_version: 0,
186	            max_frame_length: SERVER_MAX_FRAME_LENGTH,
187	            heartbeat_interval_ms: DEFAULT_HEARTBEAT_MS,
188	            result: 1,
189	        },
190	        &mut payload,
191	    );
192	    let mut frame = Vec::new();
193	    encode_transport_frame(FrameKind::HelloAck, &payload, &mut frame);
194	    let mut goaway = Vec::new();
195	    let mut goaway_payload = Vec::new();
196	    encode_goaway(1, &mut goaway_payload);
197	    encode_transport_frame(FrameKind::Goaway, &goaway_payload, &mut goaway);
198	    stream.write_all(&frame)?;
199	    stream.write_all(&goaway)?;
200	    stream.flush()
201	}
202	
203	fn reader_loop(
204	    mut stream: TcpStream,
205	    ingress: Producer,
206	    input_drained: Arc<AtomicBool>,
207	    max_frame_length: u32,
208	) -> io::Result<()> {
209	    loop {
210	        let frame = match read_frame_eof(&mut stream, max_frame_length)? {
211	            Some(frame) => frame,
212	            None => {
213	                input_drained.store(true, Ordering::Release);
214	                return Ok(());
215	            }
216	        };
217	        match frame.kind {
218	            FrameKind::Record => {
219	                let ingress_ns = now_mono_ns();
220	                match ingress.push(&frame.payload, ingress_ns) {
221	                    Ok(()) => {}
222	                    Err(RingError::TooLarge { .. }) => {
223	                        input_drained.store(true, Ordering::Release);
224	                        return Err(io::Error::new(
225	                            ErrorKind::InvalidData,
226	                            "record exceeds slot",
227	                        ));
228	                    }
229	                    Err(err) => {
230	                        input_drained.store(true, Ordering::Release);
231	                        return Err(io::Error::other(format!("{err:?}")));
232	                    }
233	                }
234	            }
235	            FrameKind::Heartbeat => {}
236	            _ => {
237	                return Err(io::Error::new(
238	                    ErrorKind::InvalidData,
239	                    "unexpected client frame",
240	                ))
241	            }
242	        }
243	    }
244	}
245	
246	fn writer_loop(
247	    mut stream: TcpStream,
248	    ack_ring: Consumer,
249	    writer_done: Arc<AtomicBool>,
250	    heartbeat_interval_ms: u32,
251	) -> io::Result<()> {
252	    let heartbeat_interval = Duration::from_millis(u64::from(heartbeat_interval_ms.max(1)));
253	    let mut next_heartbeat = Instant::now() + heartbeat_interval;
254	    loop {
255	        match ack_ring.try_pop() {
256	            Ok(item) => {
257	                let mut frame = Vec::new();
258	                encode_transport_frame(FrameKind::Record, &item.bytes, &mut frame);
259	                stream.write_all(&frame)?;
260	                stream.flush()?;
261	            }
262	            Err(RingError::Empty) if writer_done.load(Ordering::Acquire) => break,
263	            Err(RingError::Empty) => {
264	                if Instant::now() >= next_heartbeat {
265	                    let mut frame = Vec::new();
266	                    encode_transport_frame(FrameKind::Heartbeat, &[], &mut frame);
267	                    stream.write_all(&frame)?;
268	                    stream.flush()?;
269	                    next_heartbeat = Instant::now() + heartbeat_interval;
270	                } else {
271	                    thread::sleep(Duration::from_micros(50));
272	                }
273	            }
274	            Err(err) => return Err(io::Error::other(format!("{err:?}"))),
275	        }
276	    }
277	    stream.flush()?;
278	    stream.shutdown(Shutdown::Write)
279	}
280	
281	fn read_frame(stream: &mut TcpStream, max_frame_length: u32) -> io::Result<TransportFrame> {
282	    read_frame_eof(stream, max_frame_length)?
283	        .ok_or_else(|| io::Error::new(ErrorKind::UnexpectedEof, "connection closed"))
284	}
285	
286	fn read_frame_eof(
287	    stream: &mut TcpStream,
288	    max_frame_length: u32,
289	) -> io::Result<Option<TransportFrame>> {
290	    let mut len = [0u8; 4];
291	    let mut read = 0usize;
292	    while read < len.len() {
293	        match stream.read(&mut len[read..])? {
294	            0 if read == 0 => return Ok(None),
295	            0 => {
296	                return Err(io::Error::new(
297	                    ErrorKind::UnexpectedEof,
298	                    "partial frame length",
299	                ))
300	            }
301	            n => read += n,
302	        }
303	    }
304	    let frame_length = u32::from_le_bytes(len);
305	    if frame_length == 0 || frame_length > max_frame_length {
306	        return Err(io::Error::new(ErrorKind::InvalidData, "bad frame length"));
307	    }
308	    let mut payload = vec![0u8; frame_length as usize];
309	    stream.read_exact(&mut payload)?;
310	    let kind = wire::FrameKind::from_byte(payload[0])
311	        .map_err(|_| io::Error::new(ErrorKind::InvalidData, "bad frame kind"))?;
312	    Ok(Some(TransportFrame {
313	        kind,
314	        payload: payload[1..].to_vec(),
315	    }))
316	}
317	
318	fn publish_descriptor(config: &RuntimeConfig) -> io::Result<()> {
319	    let metrics = config.run_dir.join("metrics.json");
320	    let latency = config.run_dir.join("publish-ts.bin");
321	    let telemetry = if config.telemetry_enabled {
322	        format!(
323	            "{{\"metrics_dump\":\"{}\",\"publish_timestamps\":\"{}\"}}",
324	            escape_json(&metrics.to_string_lossy()),
325	            escape_json(&latency.to_string_lossy())
326	        )
327	    } else {
328	        "null".to_string()
329	    };
330	    // `drain` is a snake_case object {mode, handle} per boundary-adapter.md obligation 1
331	    // (handle is null for half_close). The benchmarker reads only the telemetry pointers.
332	    let descriptor = format!(
333	        "{{\"ordp_endpoint\":\"{}\",\"outbox_dir\":\"{}\",\"outbox_glob\":\"*.obox\",\"drain\":{{\"mode\":\"half_close\",\"handle\":null}},\"sanctioned_divergence\":false,\"state_probe\":null,\"telemetry\":{}}}\n",
334	        escape_json(&config.ordp_endpoint),
335	        escape_json(&config.run_dir.to_string_lossy()),
336	        telemetry
337	    );
338	    let tmp = config
339	        .run_dir
340	        .join(format!(".descriptor.{}.tmp", crate::telemetry::now_ns()));
341	    std::fs::write(&tmp, descriptor)?;
342	    std::fs::rename(tmp, config.run_dir.join("descriptor.json"))
343	}
344	
345	fn escape_json(s: &str) -> String {
346	    s.replace('\\', "\\\\").replace('"', "\\\"")
347	}
348	
349	fn join_io(handle: thread::JoinHandle<io::Result<()>>, name: &'static str) -> io::Result<()> {
350	    match handle.join() {
351	        Ok(result) => result,
352	        Err(_) => Err(io::Error::other(format!("{name} thread panicked"))),
353	    }
354	}
355
  16. assistantassistant2026-06-27 20:47:50 UTC
    tool: Read
    {
  "file_path": "/home/grace/.herdr/worktrees/synfinex/single-sequencer-consolidated/experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw/src/outbox.rs"
}
  17. useruser2026-06-27 20:47:50 UTC
    tool result
    1	use crate::wire;
2	use std::fs::File;
3	use std::io::{BufWriter, Write};
4	use std::path::Path;
5	
6	pub struct Outbox {
7	    writer: BufWriter<File>,
8	    next_frame_seq: u64,
9	}
10	
11	impl Outbox {
12	    pub fn create(path: &Path) -> std::io::Result<Self> {
13	        if let Some(parent) = path.parent() {
14	            std::fs::create_dir_all(parent)?;
15	        }
16	        let file = File::create(path)?;
17	        let mut writer = BufWriter::new(file);
18	        writer.write_all(&wire::write_obox_header(1))?;
19	        Ok(Self {
20	            writer,
21	            next_frame_seq: 1,
22	        })
23	    }
24	
25	    pub fn append_response_frame(&mut self, record_bytes: &[u8]) -> std::io::Result<()> {
26	        let mut frame = Vec::new();
27	        wire::encode_log_frame(self.next_frame_seq, record_bytes, &mut frame);
28	        self.write_frame(frame)
29	    }
30	
31	    pub fn append_response_record(
32	        &mut self,
33	        record: &wire::records::Record,
34	    ) -> std::io::Result<()> {
35	        let mut frame = Vec::new();
36	        wire::encode_log_frame_record(
37	            self.next_frame_seq,
38	            |frame| wire::records::encode_record(record, frame),
39	            &mut frame,
40	        );
41	        self.write_frame(frame)
42	    }
43	
44	    fn write_frame(&mut self, frame: Vec<u8>) -> std::io::Result<()> {
45	        self.next_frame_seq = self
46	            .next_frame_seq
47	            .checked_add(1)
48	            .expect("outbox frame sequence overflow");
49	        self.writer.write_all(&frame)
50	    }
51	
52	    pub fn flush(&mut self) -> std::io::Result<()> {
53	        self.writer.flush()
54	    }
55	}
56	
57	#[cfg(test)]
58	mod tests {
59	    use super::*;
60	    use crate::wire::records::{encode_record, ClockTickRequest, Record};
61	    use std::io::Read;
62	
63	    #[test]
64	    fn header_is_written_and_validates() {
65	        let path = std::env::temp_dir().join(format!(
66	            "spine-obox-test-{}-{}.obox",
67	            std::process::id(),
68	            crate::telemetry::now_ns()
69	        ));
70	        {
71	            let mut outbox = Outbox::create(&path).unwrap();
72	            outbox.flush().unwrap();
73	        }
74	        let mut bytes = Vec::new();
75	        File::open(&path).unwrap().read_to_end(&mut bytes).unwrap();
76	        assert_eq!(bytes.len(), 64);
77	        assert_eq!(wire::validate_obox_header(&bytes).unwrap(), 1);
78	        let _ = std::fs::remove_file(path);
79	    }
80	
81	    #[test]
82	    fn append_response_frame_assigns_contiguous_frame_sequence() {
83	        let path = std::env::temp_dir().join(format!(
84	            "spine-obox-seq-test-{}-{}.obox",
85	            std::process::id(),
86	            crate::telemetry::now_ns()
87	        ));
88	        let mut record = Vec::new();
89	        encode_record(
90	            &Record::ClockTickRequest(ClockTickRequest { now: 123 }),
91	            &mut record,
92	        );
93	        {
94	            let mut outbox = Outbox::create(&path).unwrap();
95	            outbox.append_response_frame(&record).unwrap();
96	            outbox.append_response_frame(&record).unwrap();
97	            outbox.flush().unwrap();
98	        }
99	        let mut bytes = Vec::new();
100	        File::open(&path).unwrap().read_to_end(&mut bytes).unwrap();
101	        assert_eq!(wire::validate_obox_header(&bytes[..64]).unwrap(), 1);
102	        let first_len = u32::from_le_bytes(bytes[64..68].try_into().unwrap()) as usize;
103	        let first_end = 68 + first_len;
104	        assert_eq!(
105	            wire::validate_log_frame(&bytes[64..first_end]).unwrap().0,
106	            1
107	        );
108	        assert_eq!(wire::validate_log_frame(&bytes[first_end..]).unwrap().0, 2);
109	        let _ = std::fs::remove_file(path);
110	    }
111	}
112

    tool result
    1	use std::cell::{Cell, UnsafeCell};
2	use std::marker::PhantomData;
3	use std::sync::atomic::{AtomicUsize, Ordering};
4	use std::sync::Arc;
5	use std::thread;
6	
7	pub const SLOT_CAP: usize = 4096;
8	
9	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
10	pub enum RingError {
11	    Full,
12	    Empty,
13	    TooLarge { len: usize, cap: usize },
14	}
15	
16	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
17	pub struct RingItem {
18	    pub bytes: Vec<u8>,
19	    pub ingress_ns: u64,
20	}
21	
22	struct Slot {
23	    len: usize,
24	    ingress_ns: u64,
25	    bytes: [u8; SLOT_CAP],
26	}
27	
28	impl Slot {
29	    fn empty() -> Self {
30	        Self {
31	            len: 0,
32	            ingress_ns: 0,
33	            bytes: [0; SLOT_CAP],
34	        }
35	    }
36	}
37	
38	struct Inner {
39	    slots: Box<[UnsafeCell<Slot>]>,
40	    mask: usize,
41	    head: AtomicUsize,
42	    tail: AtomicUsize,
43	}
44	
45	unsafe impl Send for Inner {}
46	unsafe impl Sync for Inner {}
47	
48	pub struct Producer {
49	    inner: Arc<Inner>,
50	    _not_sync: PhantomData<Cell<()>>,
51	}
52	
53	pub struct Consumer {
54	    inner: Arc<Inner>,
55	    _not_sync: PhantomData<Cell<()>>,
56	}
57	
58	pub fn channel(capacity: usize) -> (Producer, Consumer) {
59	    assert!(capacity.is_power_of_two());
60	    assert!(capacity > 0);
61	    let slots = (0..capacity)
62	        .map(|_| UnsafeCell::new(Slot::empty()))
63	        .collect::<Vec<_>>()
64	        .into_boxed_slice();
65	    let inner = Arc::new(Inner {
66	        slots,
67	        mask: capacity - 1,
68	        head: AtomicUsize::new(0),
69	        tail: AtomicUsize::new(0),
70	    });
71	    (
72	        Producer {
73	            inner: Arc::clone(&inner),
74	            _not_sync: PhantomData,
75	        },
76	        Consumer {
77	            inner,
78	            _not_sync: PhantomData,
79	        },
80	    )
81	}
82	
83	impl Producer {
84	    pub fn try_push(&self, bytes: &[u8], ingress_ns: u64) -> Result<(), RingError> {
85	        if bytes.len() > SLOT_CAP {
86	            return Err(RingError::TooLarge {
87	                len: bytes.len(),
88	                cap: SLOT_CAP,
89	            });
90	        }
91	        let tail = self.inner.tail.load(Ordering::Relaxed);
92	        let head = self.inner.head.load(Ordering::Acquire);
93	        if tail.wrapping_sub(head) == self.capacity() {
94	            return Err(RingError::Full);
95	        }
96	        let idx = tail & self.inner.mask;
97	        // The endpoint split guarantees this is the only producer mutating this slot.
98	        let slot = unsafe { &mut *self.inner.slots[idx].get() };
99	        slot.len = bytes.len();
100	        slot.ingress_ns = ingress_ns;
101	        slot.bytes[..bytes.len()].copy_from_slice(bytes);
102	        self.inner
103	            .tail
104	            .store(tail.wrapping_add(1), Ordering::Release);
105	        Ok(())
106	    }
107	
108	    pub fn push(&self, bytes: &[u8], ingress_ns: u64) -> Result<(), RingError> {
109	        loop {
110	            match self.try_push(bytes, ingress_ns) {
111	                Err(RingError::Full) => thread::yield_now(),
112	                other => return other,
113	            }
114	        }
115	    }
116	
117	    fn capacity(&self) -> usize {
118	        self.inner.slots.len()
119	    }
120	}
121	
122	impl Consumer {
123	    pub fn is_empty(&self) -> bool {
124	        self.inner.head.load(Ordering::Acquire) == self.inner.tail.load(Ordering::Acquire)
125	    }
126	
127	    pub fn try_pop(&self) -> Result<RingItem, RingError> {
128	        let head = self.inner.head.load(Ordering::Relaxed);
129	        let tail = self.inner.tail.load(Ordering::Acquire);
130	        if head == tail {
131	            return Err(RingError::Empty);
132	        }
133	        let idx = head & self.inner.mask;
134	        // The endpoint split guarantees this is the only consumer reading this slot.
135	        let slot = unsafe { &*self.inner.slots[idx].get() };
136	        let out = RingItem {
137	            bytes: slot.bytes[..slot.len].to_vec(),
138	            ingress_ns: slot.ingress_ns,
139	        };
140	        self.inner
141	            .head
142	            .store(head.wrapping_add(1), Ordering::Release);
143	        Ok(out)
144	    }
145	
146	    pub fn pop(&self) -> RingItem {
147	        loop {
148	            if let Ok(item) = self.try_pop() {
149	                return item;
150	            }
151	            thread::yield_now();
152	        }
153	    }
154	}
155	
156	#[cfg(test)]
157	mod tests {
158	    use super::*;
159	
160	    #[test]
161	    fn full_empty_and_fifo_are_preserved() {
162	        let (producer, consumer) = channel(2);
163	        assert_eq!(consumer.try_pop().unwrap_err(), RingError::Empty);
164	        producer.try_push(b"one", 1).unwrap();
165	        producer.try_push(b"two", 2).unwrap();
166	        assert_eq!(producer.try_push(b"three", 3).unwrap_err(), RingError::Full);
167	        assert_eq!(consumer.try_pop().unwrap().bytes, b"one");
168	        assert_eq!(consumer.try_pop().unwrap().bytes, b"two");
169	        assert_eq!(consumer.try_pop().unwrap_err(), RingError::Empty);
170	    }
171	
172	    #[test]
173	    fn wrap_around_keeps_order() {
174	        let (producer, consumer) = channel(4);
175	        for i in 0u8..4 {
176	            producer.try_push(&[i], u64::from(i)).unwrap();
177	        }
178	        for i in 0u8..2 {
179	            assert_eq!(consumer.try_pop().unwrap().bytes, vec![i]);
180	        }
181	        producer.try_push(&[4], 4).unwrap();
182	        producer.try_push(&[5], 5).unwrap();
183	        for i in 2u8..6 {
184	            assert_eq!(consumer.try_pop().unwrap().bytes, vec![i]);
185	        }
186	    }
187	
188	    #[test]
189	    fn two_threads_transfer_in_order() {
190	        let (producer, consumer) = channel(8);
191	        let handle = std::thread::spawn(move || {
192	            for i in 0u16..1000 {
193	                producer.push(&i.to_le_bytes(), u64::from(i)).unwrap();
194	            }
195	        });
196	        for i in 0u16..1000 {
197	            let item = consumer.pop();
198	            assert_eq!(item.bytes, i.to_le_bytes());
199	            assert_eq!(item.ingress_ns, u64::from(i));
200	        }
201	        handle.join().unwrap();
202	    }
203	}
204

    # Run Log - rust-raw phase timing instrument

## Phase-timing cross-check instrument - DONE

Implemented a compile-time-gated `phase-timing` Cargo feature, off by default. The
feature adds a thread-local rdtscp cycle accumulator, coarse accept-path brackets,
clean nested order-fold checkpoints, and a `FAST_PATH_MODE=phase-timing` bench mode.

Verification:

```text
$ cargo test --lib
   Compiling spine v0.1.0 (/home/grace/.herdr/worktrees/synfinex/dre-infinex-dre-single-sequencer-rust-raw/experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw)
    Finished `test` profile [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 1.71s
     Running unittests src/lib.rs (target/debug/deps/spine-4d02d7f5e95dbfac)

running 111 tests
test result: ok. 111 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out; finished in 0.00s

$ cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings
   Compiling spine v0.1.0 (/home/grace/.herdr/worktrees/synfinex/dre-infinex-dre-single-sequencer-rust-raw/experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw)
    Finished `dev` profile [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 3.90s

$ cargo clippy --all-targets --features phase-timing -- -D warnings
   Compiling spine v0.1.0 (/home/grace/.herdr/worktrees/synfinex/dre-infinex-dre-single-sequencer-rust-raw/experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw)
    Finished `dev` profile [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 2.75s

$ cargo build
   Compiling spine v0.1.0 (/home/grace/.herdr/worktrees/synfinex/dre-infinex-dre-single-sequencer-rust-raw/experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw)
    Finished `dev` profile [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 1.22s

$ FAST_PATH_PHASE_OPS=1000 FAST_PATH_MODE=phase-timing cargo bench --bench fast_path --features phase-timing -- --quiet
   Compiling spine v0.1.0 (/home/grace/.herdr/worktrees/synfinex/dre-infinex-dre-single-sequencer-rust-raw/experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw)
    Finished `bench` profile [optimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 20.63s
     Running benches/fast_path.rs (target/release/deps/fast_path-bc282cafe0a557d3)
phase_timing depth=1000 warmup=100000 measured=1000 seed=1 clock=rdtscp floor_cycles=45 floor_ns=10.019 tsc_freq_hz=4491558136.154
phase_timing_note rdtscp brackets inflate absolute phase numbers; use this for ranking and cross-checking, not as the latency gate
build_config target_cpu=znver4 opt_level=3 lto=fat codegen_units=1 panic=unwind rustc_version=rustc 1.96.0 (ac68faa20 2026-05-25)

fold,1000,4346383.684,4346.384,97.00
dedup,2000,100668.852,100.669,2.25
codec,1000,33753.320,33.753,0.75
phase_timing_cross_check whole_amortized_ns_op=4722.509 coarse_sum_ns_op=4480.806 residual_ns_op=241.703
phase_timing_nested_fold_table phase,segments,summed_ns,mean_ns_op,share_of_fold_total_pct
emit,2560,177563.103,177.563,4.09
admit_margin,556,77124.684,77.125,1.77
match,556,61535.439,61.535,1.42
phase_timing_rejects 0
```

Bracket points:

- `codec`: `src/sequencer.rs:181` through `src/sequencer.rs:188`, around
  `records::decode_record(record_bytes)`.
- `dedup`: `src/sequencer.rs:190` through `src/sequencer.rs:201`, around
  `record_info_from(&record)` and the dedup lookup; `src/sequencer.rs:228` through
  `src/sequencer.rs:238`, around the miss insert.
- `fold`: `src/sequencer.rs:216` through `src/sequencer.rs:226` for dedup-keyed
  misses, and `src/sequencer.rs:246` through `src/sequencer.rs:250` for records
  without a dedup key.
- `admit_margin`: `src/sequencer.rs:1169` through `src/sequencer.rs:1187`, around
  `admit_order`.
- `match`: `src/sequencer.rs:1232` through `src/sequencer.rs:1241`, around
  `submit_with_rollback`.
- `emit`: `src/sequencer.rs:3059` through `src/sequencer.rs:3067`, around response
  encoding and `append_response_frame`.

Left interleaved: position folding, account mutation, rollback snapshots, and margin
response selection remain inside `fold`. They are not separated because response
emission and state mutation are interwoven through the order fold.

## Client-order-id hashmap swap - DONE

Changed the client-order-id index from a `BTreeMap` to a std `HashMap` using the
existing `FxHasher` via `BuildHasherDefault`. The map remains lookup-only for engine
behavior: call sites use point lookups, insertion, removal, containment checks, and
content cloning. Rollback still records client-order-id preimages in its ordered
vector and replays that vector; no response emission iterates the hashmap.

The change is byte-identical by construction because hash bucket order is not
=== wire/mod head ===
pub mod records;

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests;

pub const SCHEMA_VERSION: u16 = 1;
pub const HEADER_LEN: usize = 8;
pub const GROUP_HEADER_LEN: usize = 8;
pub const OBOX_MAGIC: [u8; 4] = *b"OBOX";
pub const HELLO_MAGIC: [u8; 4] = *b"ORDP";

#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum WireError {
    ShortRead,
    TrailingBytes,
    BadBool(u8),
    BadEnum { name: &'static str, value: u8 },
    BadReserved,
    BadSchemaVersion(u16),
    BadFrameKind(u8),
    BadFrameLength,
    BadMagic,
    CrcMismatch,
}

pub type Result<T> = std::result::Result<T, WireError>;

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub struct Header {
    pub schema_version: u16,
    pub template_id: u16,
    pub root_block_length: u16,
    pub reserved: u16,
}

impl Header {
    pub fn write(template_id: u16, root_block_length: u16, out: &mut Vec<u8>) {
        write_u16(SCHEMA_VERSION, out);
        write_u16(template_id, out);
        write_u16(root_block_length, out);
        write_u16(0, out);
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct Reader<'a> {
    bytes: &'a [u8],
    pos: usize,
}

impl<'a> Reader<'a> {
    pub fn new(bytes: &'a [u8]) -> Self {
        Self { bytes, pos: 0 }
    }

    pub fn remaining(&self) -> usize {
        self.bytes.len().saturating_sub(self.pos)
    }

    pub fn consumed(&self) -> usize {
        self.pos
    }

    pub fn bytes(&mut self, len: usize) -> Result<&'a [u8]> {
        let end = self.pos.checked_add(len).ok_or(WireError::ShortRead)?;
        if end > self.bytes.len() {
            return Err(WireError::ShortRead);
        }
        let out = &self.bytes[self.pos..end];
        self.pos = end;
        Ok(out)
    }

    pub fn advance(&mut self, len: usize) -> Result<()> {
        self.bytes(len).map(|_| ())
    }

    pub fn u8(&mut self) -> Result<u8> {
        Ok(self.bytes(1)?[0])
    }

  26. assistantassistant2026-06-27 20:48:03 UTC
    tool: Read
    {
  "file_path": "/home/grace/.herdr/worktrees/synfinex/single-sequencer-consolidated/experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw/src/sequencer.rs",

}

    tool result
    290	impl Default for Sequencer {
291	    fn default() -> Self {
292	        Self::new()
293	    }
294	}
295	
296	pub fn run_core(
297	    ingress: Consumer,
298	    ack_ring: Producer,
299	    input_drained: Arc<AtomicBool>,
300	    writer_done: Arc<AtomicBool>,
301	    mut outbox: Outbox,
302	    mut telemetry: Telemetry,
303	) -> std::io::Result<()> {
304	    let mut sequencer = Sequencer::new();
305	    loop {
306	        match ingress.try_pop() {
307	            Ok(item) => {
308	                let ack = sequencer.accept_with_sink(&item.bytes, &mut outbox)?;
309	                if ack.disposition == AckKind::Sequenced {
310	                    telemetry.record_seq(ack.seq, item.ingress_ns, now_mono_ns());
311	                }
312	                let ack_bytes = encode_ack_record(&ack);
313	                ack_ring
314	                    .push(&ack_bytes, 0)
315	                    .map_err(|err| std::io::Error::other(format!("{err:?}")))?;
316	            }
317	            Err(RingError::Empty) if input_drained.load(Ordering::Acquire) => break,
318	            Err(RingError::Empty) => thread::sleep(Duration::from_micros(50)),
319	            Err(err) => {
320	                return Err(std::io::Error::other(format!(
321	                    "ingress ring pop failed: {err:?}"
322	                )));
323	            }
324	        }
325	    }
326	    outbox.flush()?;
327	    telemetry.dump()?;
328	    writer_done.store(true, Ordering::Release);
329	    Ok(())
330	}
331	
332	pub fn encode_ack_record(ack: &Ack) -> Vec<u8> {
333	    let mut out = Vec::new();
334	    records::encode_record(&Record::Ack(ack.clone()), &mut out);
335	    out
336	}
337	
338	fn sequenced_ack(idem: Idem, seq: u64) -> Ack {
339	    Ack {
340	        idem,
341	        seq,
342	        disposition: AckKind::Sequenced,
343	        shed_reason: None,
344	    }
345	}
346	
347	fn shed_malformed_ack(idem: Idem) -> Ack {
348	    Ack {
349	        idem,
350	        seq: 0,
351	        disposition: AckKind::Shed,
352	        shed_reason: Some(ShedReason::Malformed),
353	    }
354	}
355	
356	pub trait ResponseSink {
357	    fn append_response_frame(&mut self, record_bytes: &[u8]) -> std::io::Result<()>;
358	
359	    fn append_response_record(&mut self, record: &Record) -> std::io::Result<()> {
360	        let mut bytes = Vec::new();
361	        records::encode_record(record, &mut bytes);
362	        self.append_response_frame(&bytes)
363	    }
364	}
365	
366	impl ResponseSink for Outbox {
367	    fn append_response_frame(&mut self, record_bytes: &[u8]) -> std::io::Result<()> {
368	        Outbox::append_response_frame(self, record_bytes)
369	    }
370	
371	    fn append_response_record(&mut self, record: &Record) -> std::io::Result<()> {
372	        Outbox::append_response_record(self, record)
373	    }
374	}
375	
376	struct NullResponseSink;
377	
378	impl ResponseSink for NullResponseSink {
379	    fn append_response_frame(&mut self, _record_bytes: &[u8]) -> std::io::Result<()> {
380	        Ok(())
381	    }
382	
383	    fn append_response_record(&mut self, _record: &Record) -> std::io::Result<()> {
384	        Ok(())
385	    }
386	}
387	
388	fn frame_range(start: usize, end: usize) -> (u32, u32) {
389	    (
390	        u32::try_from(start).expect("dedup frame index arena exceeds u32"),
391	        u32::try_from(end).expect("dedup frame index arena exceeds u32"),
392	    )
393	}
394	
395	struct ArenaResponseSink<'a, S: ResponseSink> {
396	    inner: &'a mut S,
397	    byte_arena: &'a mut Vec<u8>,
398	    frame_arena: &'a mut Vec<FrameSpan>,
399	}
400	
401	impl<'a, S: ResponseSink> ArenaResponseSink<'a, S> {
402	    fn new(
403	        inner: &'a mut S,
404	        byte_arena: &'a mut Vec<u8>,
405	        frame_arena: &'a mut Vec<FrameSpan>,
406	    ) -> Self {
407	        Self {
408	            inner,
409	            byte_arena,
410	            frame_arena,
411	        }
412	    }
413	}
414	
415	impl<S: ResponseSink> ResponseSink for ArenaResponseSink<'_, S> {
416	    fn append_response_frame(&mut self, record_bytes: &[u8]) -> std::io::Result<()> {
417	        self.inner.append_response_frame(record_bytes)?;
418	        let start = u32::try_from(self.byte_arena.len()).expect("dedup byte arena exceeds u32");
419	        let len = u32::try_from(record_bytes.len()).expect("dedup response frame exceeds u32");
420	        self.byte_arena.extend_from_slice(record_bytes);
421	        self.frame_arena.push(FrameSpan { start, len });
422	        Ok(())
423	    }
424	
425	    fn append_response_record(&mut self, record: &Record) -> std::io::Result<()> {
426	        let start = self.byte_arena.len();
427	        records::encode_record(record, self.byte_arena);
428	        let end = self.byte_arena.len();
429	        self.inner
430	            .append_response_frame(&self.byte_arena[start..end])?;
431	        self.frame_arena.push(FrameSpan {
432	            start: u32::try_from(start).expect("dedup byte arena exceeds u32"),
433	            len: u32::try_from(end - start).expect("dedup response frame exceeds u32"),
434	        });
435	        Ok(())
436	    }
437	}
438	
439	const ACCOUNT_MARKET_VEC_RESERVED: usize = 4;
440	
441	#[derive(Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
442	struct Account {
443	    usdt_quantity: i64,
444	    status: records::AccountStatus,
445	    positions: Vec<(u32, Position)>,
446	    resting_order_count: u32,
447	    resting_by_market: Vec<(u32, RestingMarketAggregate)>,
448	}
449

codex-cli 0.142.2
---help---
Run Codex non-interactively

Usage: codex exec [OPTIONS] [PROMPT]
       codex exec [OPTIONS] <COMMAND> [ARGS]

Commands:
  resume  Resume a previous session by id or pick the most recent with --last
  review  Run a code review against the current repository
  help    Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)

Arguments:
  [PROMPT]
          Initial instructions for the agent. If not provided as an argument (or if `-` is used),
          instructions are read from stdin. If stdin is piped and a prompt is also provided, stdin
          is appended as a `<stdin>` block

Options:
  -c, --config <key=value>
          Override a configuration value that would otherwise be loaded from `~/.codex/config.toml`.
          Use a dotted path (`foo.bar.baz`) to override nested values. The `value` portion is parsed
          as TOML. If it fails to parse as TOML, the raw string is used as a literal.
          
          Examples: - `-c model="o3"` - `-c 'sandbox_permissions=["disk-full-read-access"]'` - `-c
          shell_environment_policy.inherit=all`

      --enable <FEATURE>
          Enable a feature (repeatable). Equivalent to `-c features.<name>=true`

      --disable <FEATURE>
          Disable a feature (repeatable). Equivalent to `-c features.<name>=false`

#![allow(clippy::float_arithmetic)]

use criterion::{black_box, BatchSize, Criterion};
use spine::alloc_counter::{
    allocation_trace_reset, allocation_trace_snapshot, allocation_trace_start,
    allocation_trace_stop, snapshot,
};

    OrderRequest, OrderSide, OrderType, Record, TimeInForce,
};
use std::env;
use std::fmt::Write as _;
use std::fs;
use std::process::Command;
use std::time::Instant;

mod tsc_clock {
    // REFERENCE measurement core — rdtscp per-op clock (see fast-path-profiling-harness.md).
    // Benchmarker-owned, engine-agnostic, correctness-critical. A rust builder drops this into the
    // sustained bench and replaces Instant::now() per-op timing with rd_start()/rd_end(); the raw u64
    // cycle-delta goes into the pre-sized Vec<u64> (one store, no convert in the hot loop). After the run,
    // TscClock::net_ns(raw) subtracts the floor and converts cycles->ns. Validated on the EPYC 4564P ref
    // host: invariant_tsc=1, CPUID.15H empty (calibration required), TSC 4491.55 MHz, floor 45cyc/10.0ns.
    //
    // x86_64 only; gate the module with #[cfg(target_arch = "x86_64")].

    #[cfg(target_arch = "x86_64")]
    use core::arch::x86_64::{__cpuid, __rdtscp, _mm_lfence, _rdtsc};
    use std::fs;
    use std::time::{Duration, Instant};

    /// START of the timed region: lfence; rdtsc; lfence. Pair with rd_end() around exactly the op.
    #[cfg(target_arch = "x86_64")]
    #[inline(always)]
    pub fn rd_start() -> u64 {
        unsafe {
            _mm_lfence();
  35. assistantassistant2026-06-27 20:48:34 UTC

    I now have a solid architectural map. Let me write one shared brief and dispatch three independent reviewers: a **Codex** agent (whole-architecture, via `codex exec`) and two **Claude** agents (one whole-architecture for direct cross-engine contrast, one deep-dive on the durable-journal + zero-copy IO design).   41. assistantassistant2026-06-27 20:49:23 UTC
    tool: Write
    {
  "file_path": "/tmp/claude-1003/-home-grace--herdr-worktrees-synfinex-single-sequencer-consolidated/87040bc7-121c-446a-af94-40582ff6038e/scratchpad/arch-brief.md",
  "content": "# Architecture review brief — rust-raw single-sequencer (\"spine\")\n\n## Your job\nProduce a **prioritized architecture recommendation** for squeezing more performance\nout of this matching-engine sequencer, with NO compromise on correctness, performance,\nor latency. Two priorities dominate everything else:\n\n1. **Durability + replay**: the response log (`.obox`) must become a committed journal\n   with a real durability story (fsync / group commit / crash safety) AND a corresponding\n   **replay/recovery** path that reconstructs engine state from the journal on restart.\n2. **Zero-copy across the board**: network stack → rings → engine → disk. Eliminate the\n   per-message copies and heap allocations on the hot path.\n\nDo NOT write implementation code. Produce a design/recommendation document: concrete\nmechanisms, the tradeoffs, ordering of work, and — critically — how each change keeps the\ngolden fixtures and benchmarks honest (what could silently break correctness or the\nlatency gate, and how you'd guard it).\n\n## Where things are\nRepo root for this experiment: `experiments/single-sequencer/`\nEngine under review: `experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw/` (crate name `spine`).\nReference worth reading: `experiments/single-sequencer/docs/tigerbeetle-harvest.md`,\n`docs/system-design.md`, `contract/boundary-adapter.md`, and the golden fixtures under\n`contract/harness/golden/frozen-obox/`.\n\n## Current architecture (verified)\n- 3-thread pipeline in `src/net.rs` + `src/sequencer.rs`:\n  `reader_loop` (TCP → ingress ring) → `run_core` (sequencer fold → outbox + ack ring)\n  → `writer_loop` (ack ring → TCP).\n- `src/ring.rs`: SPSC ring, fixed `SLOT_CAP = 4096`-byte slots. `try_push` **copies**\n  the payload into the slot; `try_pop` **allocates a fresh `Vec` and copies out**\n  (`slot.bytes[..len].to_vec()`). So every message is copied+allocated on BOTH the\n  ingress and ack hops.\n- `src/net.rs`: `read_frame_eof` allocates `vec![0u8; len]` per frame and then\n  `payload[1..].to_vec()` again. `writer_loop` builds a fresh `Vec` per frame and does\n  `write_all` + `flush` per message (syscall per message; no vectored I/O, no batching,\n  no TCP_NODELAY set explicitly).\n- `src/outbox.rs`: `BufWriter<File>`; each `append_*` allocates a fresh `Vec`, encodes a\n  log frame, writes. **`flush()` is called only once, after the run_core loop exits**\n  (`src/sequencer.rs:326`). **There is no `fsync` anywhere** → today the journal is not\n  durable, and there is **no replay/recovery path** that reads `.obox` back to rebuild state.\n- `src/sequencer.rs` (~6800 lines): heavily optimized compute core — dedup arenas, slab\n  storage, in-place fold with rollback snapshots, FxHasher maps. This part is already fast;\n  treat it as mostly fixed but flag anything that blocks zero-copy or durability.\n\n## Performance context (from prior runs)\n- Compute spine currently ~p99.9 3.8µs flat; target is ~1µs/order.\n- Latency gate is measured with amortized batch timing + rdtscp; p50/p99/p99.9 are the\n  gate. Tail beyond that on a shared box is descheduling noise, not engine.\n\n## Honesty harness (must stay green)\n- Golden fixtures: byte-frozen `.obox` outputs under `contract/harness/golden/frozen-obox/`\n  — any change to emitted bytes must be intentional and re-frozen, never silently diverge.\n- Benchmarks: `benches/fast_path.rs` (criterion fast-path + sustained tail), rdtscp clock.\n- `cargo test --lib`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`.\n\n## Conventions (AGENTS.md — follow in your writeup)\n- NO opaque identifiers (no D1/F2/Option-A/rung-4, no order-encoded tags). Name each\n  decision/option by a short descriptive phrase or a Named Capitalised Term that carries\n  its meaning.\n- Write current-state prose, not a changelog.\n\n## What to deliver\nA ranked set of recommendations. For each: what it changes, the mechanism, expected\nlatency/throughput/durability effect, the correctness risk and how the golden+bench\nharness catches it, and rough effort. Call out anything where durability and zero-copy\npull against each other (e.g. fsync latency vs the 1µs budget), and how you'd resolve it\n(group commit, async-commit with bounded loss window, mmap+msync, io_uring, etc.).\nBe specific to THIS codebase, not generic advice.\n"
}

Cover BOTH priorities: (1) durable committed journal + replay/recovery, (2) zero-copy across network→ring→engine→disk. Explicitly address where fsync durability and the ~1µs latency budget pull against each other and how you'd resolve it. ~1500-2500 words. Cite file:line where relevant.",

    {

  "prompt": "You are an independent staff-level systems engineer doing a DEEP technical design (not a survey) on two things only: (A) a durable committed journal with crash-safe replay/recovery, and (B) zero-copy data movement across the network→ring→engine→disk path. This is for a Rust single-sequencer matching engine (\"spine\"). Do not write production implementation code, but pseudocode/struct sketches and syscall-level specifics are welcome. Be concrete and specific to THIS codebase.\n\nRead this brief first:\n/tmp/claude-1003/-home-grace--herdr-worktrees-synfinex-single-sequencer-consolidated/87040bc7-121c-446a-af94-40582ff6038e/scratchpad/arch-brief.md\n\nThen read these before writing:\n- experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw/src/ring.rs\n- experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw/src/net.rs\n- experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw/src/outbox.rs\n- experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw/src/sequencer.rs (run_core ~290-330, ResponseSink/ArenaResponseSink ~356-437, and skim how the dedup arenas + rollback snapshots work since they affect what \"recovered state\" must contain)\n- experiments/single-sequencer/impl/rust-raw/src/wire/mod.rs and src/wire/records.rs (frame/record layout — matters for zero-copy parse and for what the journal stores)\n- experiments/single-sequencer/contract/harness/golden/frozen-obox/README.md\n- experiments/single-sequencer/docs/tigerbeetle-harvest.md\nPaths are relative to cwd /home/grace/.herdr/worktrees/synfinex/single-sequencer-consolidated.\n\nCover concretely:\nDURABILITY+REPLAY: What exactly must be journaled to deterministically reconstruct engine state (inputs vs outputs vs snapshots)? Where does the fsync boundary go relative to the ack to the client (commit-before-ack vs async-commit with a bounded-loss window)? Group-commit batching design and its interaction with the per-message ack path. Crash-consistency of the .obox format (torn writes, partial frames, checksum/CRC at frame boundaries — note wire.rs already has CRC). Recovery: detect last good frame, truncate torn tail, replay to rebuild dedup arenas + book + accounts; how to verify recovered state byte-equals a clean run (tie to golden frozen-obox). Periodic state snapshots + log truncation to bound replay time.\nZERO-COPY: Concretely how to remove the copies I flagged — ring.rs slot copy + Vec alloc on pop, net.rs per-frame vec allocs and to_vec, writer per-frame Vec + flush-per-message. Options: pass slot references/borrowed slices instead of owned Vecs, a shared byte arena / bump region the engine borrows from, mmap'd journal written in place (engine encodes responses directly into the mmap'd log region — single buffer is both the in-memory response and the durable record), io_uring for batched submit on both socket and disk, sendfile/splice from journal to socket, vectored writes. For each, the lifetime/ownership challenge in Rust and how the SPSC single-producer-single-consumer split makes it sound. Quantify rough copy/alloc count removed per message.\n\nYour output is the final deliverable returned to me (the orchestrator) — raw data, not a chat message. NO opaque identifiers (name things descriptively per the brief). Address where fsync latency fights the ~1µs budget. ~1800-2800 words. Cite file:line.",
  "subagent_type": "general-purpose",
  "run_in_background": true
}

257:fn crc32c_update(mut crc: u32, bytes: &[u8]) -> u32 {
259:        crc ^= u32::from(*byte);
261:            let mask = 0u32.wrapping_sub(crc & 1);
262:            crc = (crc >> 1) ^ (0x82F6_3B78 & mask);
265:    crc
268:pub fn crc32c(bytes: &[u8]) -> u32 {
269:    let crc = crc32c_update(0xFFFF_FFFFu32, bytes);

274:    let crc = crc32c_update(0xFFFF_FFFFu32, first);
275:    let crc = crc32c_update(crc, second);
276:    !crc

  56. useruser2026-06-27 20:50:15 UTC
    tool result
    405	    if r.remaining() != 0 {
406	        return Err(WireError::TrailingBytes);
407	    }
408	    Ok(reason)
409	}
410	
411	pub fn encode_log_frame(seq: u64, record: &[u8], out: &mut Vec<u8>) {
412	    encode_log_frame_record(seq, |out| out.extend_from_slice(record), out);
413	}
414	
415	pub fn encode_log_frame_record(
416	    seq: u64,
417	    encode_record: impl FnOnce(&mut Vec<u8>),
418	    out: &mut Vec<u8>,
419	) {
420	    let frame_start = out.len();
421	    write_u32(0, out);
422	    let seq_offset = out.len();
423	    write_u64(seq, out);
424	    let crc_offset = out.len();
425	    write_u32(0, out);
426	    let record_offset = out.len();
427	    encode_record(out);
428	    let frame_length = out.len() - frame_start - 4;
429	    out[frame_start..frame_start + 4].copy_from_slice(&(frame_length as u32).to_le_bytes());
430	    let crc = crc32c_pair(&out[seq_offset..seq_offset + 8], &out[record_offset..]);
431	    out[crc_offset..crc_offset + 4].copy_from_slice(&crc.to_le_bytes());
432	}
433	
434	pub fn validate_log_frame(input: &[u8]) -> Result<(u64, &[u8])> {
435	    let mut r = Reader::new(input);
436	    let frame_length = r.u32()? as usize;
437	    if frame_length != r.remaining() || frame_length < 12 {
438	        return Err(WireError::BadFrameLength);
439	    }
440	    let seq_offset = r.consumed();
441	    let seq = r.u64()?;
442	    let stored_crc = r.u32()?;
443	    let record = r.bytes(frame_length - 12)?;
444	    let mut crc_input = Vec::with_capacity(8 + record.len());
445	    crc_input.extend_from_slice(&input[seq_offset..seq_offset + 8]);
446	    crc_input.extend_from_slice(record);
447	    let crc = crc32c(&crc_input);
448	    if crc != stored_crc {
449	        return Err(WireError::CrcMismatch);
450	    }
451	    Ok((seq, record))
452	}
453	
454	pub fn write_obox_header(base_seq: u64) -> [u8; 64] {
455	    let mut header = [0u8; 64];
456	    header[0..4].copy_from_slice(&OBOX_MAGIC);
457	    header[4..6].copy_from_slice(&1u16.to_le_bytes());
458	    header[8..16].copy_from_slice(&base_seq.to_le_bytes());
459	    let crc = crc32c(&header[0..60]);
460	    header[60..64].copy_from_slice(&crc.to_le_bytes());
461	    header
462	}
463	
464	pub fn validate_obox_header(header: &[u8]) -> Result<u64> {
465	    if header.len() != 64 {
466	        return Err(WireError::ShortRead);
467	    }
468	    if header[0..4] != OBOX_MAGIC {
469	        return Err(WireError::BadMagic);
470	    }
471	    let mut r = Reader::new(header);
472	    r.advance(4)?;
473	    let format_version = r.u16()?;
474	    let reserved = r.u16()?;
475	    let base_seq = r.u64()?;
476	    let created_at = r.i64()?;
477	    let reserved_tail = r.bytes(36)?;
478	    let stored_crc = r.u32()?;
479	    if format_version != 1
480	        || reserved != 0
481	        || created_at != 0
482	        || reserved_tail.iter().any(|b| *b != 0)
483	    {
484	        return Err(WireError::BadReserved);
485	    }
486	    if crc32c(&header[0..60]) != stored_crc {
487	        return Err(WireError::CrcMismatch);
488	    }
489	    Ok(base_seq)

  58. assistantassistant2026-06-27 20:50:31 UTC
    The `.obox` format already has the bones for crash-safe recovery — a 64-byte CRC'd header with `base_seq`, and per-frame `crc32c(seq ‖ record)` (`src/wire/mod.rs:415-452`). That means torn-tail truncation is detectable today; the missing pieces are fsync/commit ordering, a replay/recovery reader, and whether the journal stores enough to rebuild state. Good grounding for synthesis.

Let me do a quick sanity check that codex actually started (it can choke on sandbox config), then wait for the three reviewers.

  75. assistantassistant2026-06-27 20:51:21 UTC
    Three independent architecture reviews are running in parallel:
- **Codex** (whole-architecture, via `codex exec`) — relaunched after a stdin-block hiccup
- **Claude agent #1** — whole-architecture, for direct cross-engine contrast
- **Claude agent #2** — deep technical design on the durable journal + zero-copy IO path

What I've already verified from my own read, which will anchor the synthesis:
- **Pipeline**: `reader → run_core → writer`, 3 threads over SPSC rings.
- **Copy/alloc hot spots**: `ring.rs` copies into a 4096B slot on push and `to_vec()`-allocates on pop (both ingress and ack hops); `net.rs` allocates per frame twice and flushes per message; `outbox.rs` allocates a Vec per append.
- **Durability gap**: the `.obox` journal is `flush()`-ed only at shutdown, **never fsync'd**, and there's **no replay/recovery reader**.
- **Good news for recovery**: the format already carries a CRC'd 64-byte header with `base_seq` and per-frame `crc32c(seq ‖ record)` (`src/wire/mod.rs:415-489`), so torn-tail truncation is already detectable — the foundation for crash-safe replay is half-built.

I'll synthesize a single prioritized recommendation once the reviewers return (they notify automatically). I've set a fallback check-in so the job can't stall silently.

  80. useruser2026-06-27 20:52:12 UTC
    tool result
    1	I could not re-open the files because command execution is failing before process start in this sandbox, so this review is based on the verified code facts in the brief and the named repo surfaces. The recommendations are still specific to `spine`’s current pipeline and contracts.
2	
3	**Highest Priority: Committed Journal With Replay**
4	
5	The `.obox` file should stop being “outbox bytes eventually flushed” and become the **Committed Response Journal**: the single durable source of truth for responses and restart recovery.
6	
7	Today `src/outbox.rs` writes encoded response frames through `BufWriter<File>`, and `src/sequencer.rs` flushes only after `run_core` exits. That means an acknowledged response can be lost on process crash or power loss. There is also no reader that rebuilds sequencer state from `.obox`, so even a perfectly flushed file is not yet a recovery mechanism.
8	
9	The concrete change is to split response emission into:
10	
11	- a journal encoder that produces the existing byte-frozen `.obox` frame format;
12	- a commit coordinator that batches frames and calls `fdatasync` or `fsync`;
13	- a replay reader that validates frame boundaries and CRC before feeding recovered responses back into engine state reconstruction.
14	
15	The golden fixtures should continue to assert byte identity for the encoded frame stream. Durability metadata must not silently perturb the frozen `.obox` bytes unless the project intentionally freezes a new journal format. If commit markers are needed, prefer an append-compatible trailer or a separate sidecar commit index rather than changing every response frame.
16	
17	The core rule should be: **the writer thread must not acknowledge a response as durable until the journal commit boundary containing that response has reached stable storage**. If the current TCP writer sends acknowledgements directly from the ack ring, the durable path needs a new ordering point:
18	
19	```text
20	sequencer fold -> journal append buffer -> durable commit -> ack publish -> TCP write
21	```
22	
23	For low-latency modes, this creates the main tension in the design: real `fsync` latency is not compatible with a one microsecond per-order budget. The correct resolution is not pretending `fsync` is cheap. It is to make durability mode explicit:
24	
25	- **Strict Durable Acknowledgement**: acknowledgements wait for group commit. Correct after crash, higher tail latency.
26	- **Bounded Loss Acknowledgement**: acknowledgements may be sent before `fsync`, with a documented maximum loss window. This is lower latency but not a committed-journal acknowledgement.
27	- **Replicated Commit Acknowledgement**: acknowledgements wait for quorum replication instead of local disk alone, if the broader system later supports this.
28	
29	For this engine, the first milestone should implement Strict Durable Acknowledgement even if it misses the microsecond gate. That gives the system a correct baseline. Only after that should the lower-latency commit policy be benchmarked and named honestly.
30	
31	Replay should be a first-class binary path, not a test-only parser. On startup, `spine` should open the Committed Response Journal, scan complete frames, verify CRCs using `wire/mod.rs` framing rules, stop at the first torn frame, and reconstruct enough engine state to resume deterministically. The recovery reader must distinguish:
32	
33	- clean end of file;
34	- torn final frame after crash;
35	- corrupted committed frame;
36	- schema or version mismatch;
37	- duplicate or replayed client operation.
38	
39	Correctness risk is high: replay can accidentally accept partial frames, rebuild less state than the live fold maintains, or diverge from dedup arenas and slab state. The guard is a recovery harness that runs the golden inputs through live execution, persists `.obox`, restarts from it, and then proves the next accepted order produces the same response bytes as an uninterrupted run. The existing frozen `.obox` fixtures catch byte drift; they do not by themselves prove recovery equivalence.
40	
41	Effort: large. This touches `outbox.rs`, `sequencer.rs` response plumbing, startup lifecycle, tests, and benchmark modes.
42	
43	**Next Priority: Zero-Copy Ring Ownership**
44	
45	`src/ring.rs` is currently the most obvious hot-path tax. `try_push` copies into a fixed slot and `try_pop` allocates a fresh `Vec`. That means ingress and ack both pay copy plus heap allocation per message.
46	
47	Replace the current byte-copy SPSC ring with a **Buffer Ownership Ring**. The ring should move ownership of preallocated buffers or descriptors, not payload bytes. A practical shape for this codebase is:
48	
49	```text
50	network reader owns free buffers
51	reader fills buffer from TCP
52	reader publishes buffer descriptor to ingress ring
53	sequencer borrows payload from descriptor
54	sequencer publishes response descriptor to journal/ack path
55	writer returns buffers to free pool after send
56	```
57	
58	The slots can still be fixed-capacity at first, preserving the current `SLOT_CAP = 4096` constraint, but the payload should stay in the same backing allocation across network, engine, and writer. The descriptor carries length, message kind, sequence metadata, and ownership state.
59	
60	The engine should consume `&[u8]` or a small `FrameRef` rather than `Vec<u8>`. Any place in `sequencer.rs` that requires ownership should be audited: ownership is acceptable only for data that must outlive the frame. Parsed fields should be copied into engine-owned compact state only when they are semantically stored, not merely because the pipeline API forces a `Vec`.
61	
62	Correctness risk is medium-high: descriptor reuse can create use-after-return bugs, stale lengths, and accidental mutation of bytes that are still needed for journaling or CRC. Rust can help if the API makes buffer return explicit and prevents aliasing. Avoid exposing raw mutable slices after publish. Tests should include poisoned-buffer reuse checks: after a frame is consumed, overwrite the buffer before the next stage would incorrectly read it.
63	
64	Benchmarks must separate allocation removal from compute speed. Add counters for hot-path allocations, or run with an allocator profiler in the benchmark harness. The latency gate should fail if a regression reintroduces per-message heap allocation.
65	
66	Effort: medium-large. The change is conceptually local to `ring.rs`, `net.rs`, and response plumbing, but it will expose assumptions in `sequencer.rs`.
67	
68	**Next Priority: Network Framing Without Per-Message Allocation**
69	
70	`src/net.rs` currently allocates `vec![0u8; len]`, then copies `payload[1..].to_vec()`. The writer builds another fresh `Vec`, then calls `write_all` and `flush` per message.
71	
72	The reader should parse directly into buffers supplied by the Buffer Ownership Ring. Read the frame header into a small stack buffer, reserve a ring buffer slot, then read the payload into the slot’s writable region. The command discriminator can be represented as an offset into the same buffer instead of slicing into a new `Vec`.
73	
74	The writer should use vectored I/O or a reusable output buffer. If the on-wire frame is length prefix plus payload plus CRC, `write_vectored` can send header and payload without assembling a new contiguous `Vec`. If the OS path benefits from contiguous writes, use a per-writer scratch buffer reused across messages, not per-message allocation.
75	
76	The per-message `flush` should go away unless the stream is actually wrapped in a buffered writer. For `TcpStream`, `write_all` already enters the kernel; flushing a wrapper per frame defeats batching. Set `TCP_NODELAY` explicitly so latency behavior is intentional, then benchmark both immediate small writes and bounded micro-batches.
77	
78	Correctness risk is mostly framing drift: off-by-one payload slices, CRC computed over the wrong bytes, or changed flush behavior hiding partial writes. The golden `.obox` fixtures catch response bytes, but not necessarily TCP framing. Add wire-level fixture tests for `wire/mod.rs` that freeze representative input and output frames, including maximum `SLOT_CAP`, empty payload rejection, bad CRC, and truncated frame cases.
79	
80	Effort: medium.
81	
82	**Next Priority: Journal Encoding Without Fresh Allocation**
83	
84	`src/outbox.rs` currently allocates a fresh `Vec` for every appended response. That defeats zero-copy at the disk boundary and creates allocator noise in the benchmark.
85	
86	The journal path should encode into either:
87	
88	- the same response buffer that will later be acknowledged;
89	- a preallocated per-core journal staging buffer;
90	- or a fixed ring of journal segments used by the commit coordinator.
91	
92	The best fit is probably a **Journal Segment Ring**: larger preallocated byte slabs, each containing many encoded `.obox` frames. `ResponseSink` appends frames into the current segment, and the commit coordinator writes complete segment ranges to disk using `write_all`, `write_vectored`, or eventually `io_uring`.
93	
94	This keeps the golden fixture contract intact because the encoded bytes remain the same; only the storage and flushing mechanics change. The fixture harness should compare the concatenated committed journal bytes to the frozen `.obox` files. It should not care how many internal segments were used.
95	
96	Correctness risk is torn-frame handling. Every journal frame needs enough length and CRC structure for replay to identify the last complete valid frame. If the existing `wire/mod.rs` framing already provides that, reuse it exactly. Do not invent a second checksum scheme for `.obox` unless the current CRC does not cover the bytes replay needs to trust.
97	
98	Effort: medium.
99	
100	**Next Priority: ResponseSink As The Durability Boundary**
101	
102	`ResponseSink` plumbing in `sequencer.rs` should become the narrow contract between the compute core and the outside world. The compute core should not know whether responses go to a `BufWriter`, a journal segment, a benchmark sink, or a replay verifier.
103	
104	The contract should express three facts explicitly:
105	
106	- the response bytes are encoded deterministically;
107	- the response has been staged for commit;
108	- the response is eligible for acknowledgement only after the selected commit policy permits it.
109	
110	That means the existing sink likely needs to return a response handle or commit position, not just accept bytes. The ack ring should carry committed response descriptors, not independently encoded ack payloads. This avoids a split-brain path where disk bytes and TCP bytes are separately allocated and separately encoded.
111	
112	Correctness risk is accidental reordering. If batching lets later responses commit before earlier responses, replay and client-visible ordering can diverge. The commit coordinator should publish acknowledgements in journal order, even if writes complete in larger batches.
113	
114	The golden fixtures catch byte order. Add a benchmark-mode assertion that the number of committed responses equals the number of published acknowledgements and that commit positions are monotonic.
115	
116	Effort: medium-large.
117	
118	**Next Priority: Replay-Driven Golden Fixtures**
119	
120	The current frozen `.obox` fixtures are necessary but insufficient once `.obox` becomes the Committed Response Journal. They prove emitted bytes, not crash recovery.
121	
122	Add a recovery fixture mode with the same frozen files:
123	
124	```text
125	read frozen .obox
126	validate every frame and CRC
127	rebuild sequencer state
128	run a deterministic suffix of new commands
129	compare emitted suffix responses with uninterrupted execution
130	```
131	
132	Also add crash-cut tests over generated journals. For every byte offset near frame boundaries, replay should either recover through the last complete committed frame or reject corruption deterministically. A torn final frame should be tolerated only if it is beyond the last committed boundary.
133	
134	This is how the harness stays honest: any journal format change must update both byte fixtures and replay expectations. Any optimization that skips a stored field will fail when recovered state produces a different next response.
135	
136	Effort: medium.
137	
138	**Next Priority: Benchmark Modes That Do Not Lie**
139	
140	The existing `benches/fast_path.rs` and rdtscp timing are appropriate for the compute spine, but durable I/O and zero-copy need separate gates.
141	
142	Keep the pure compute benchmark, because it protects the optimized `sequencer.rs` fold. Add distinct benchmark profiles:
143	
144	- **Hot Path Without Durable Commit**: network/ring/engine/journal staging with no `fsync`, measuring allocation removal and copy reduction.
145	- **Strict Durable Commit**: group commit enabled, measuring realistic acknowledgement latency.
146	- **Replay Throughput**: journal scan and state rebuild speed.
147	- **Writer Micro-Batch**: ack ring to TCP using vectored writes or bounded batches.
148	
149	Do not mix these into one number. The one microsecond target is plausible only for compute plus memory-resident staging, not for stable media commit. The architecture should report both “sequenced” latency and “durably acknowledged” latency.
150	
151	Correctness risk is benchmark dishonesty: accidentally timing only enqueue while the real work happens later. Every benchmark should consume the committed or written result in a way the optimizer cannot remove, and durable benchmarks should verify the file length and final sync result.
152	
153	Effort: small-medium.
154	
155	**Lower Priority: io_uring And mmap After The Contract Is Correct**
156	
157	`io_uring`, `mmap`, `msync`, direct I/O, and registered buffers are tempting, but they should come after the journal/replay contract and buffer ownership model are correct.
158	
159	For this codebase, `io_uring` is attractive once the Journal Segment Ring exists, because registered buffers line up with zero-copy segment ownership and async commit. It can reduce syscall overhead for batched writes and fsync operations. But it also complicates completion ordering, buffer lifetime, and error handling.
160	
161	`mmap` is less obviously right for the first durable journal. It can remove explicit write copies, but `msync` semantics and page-cache writeback behavior are easy to misunderstand. It also makes torn-write reasoning less direct than append-only `write` plus `fdatasync`.
162	
163	The recommended path is:
164	
165	- first use ordinary append-only file writes plus `fdatasync`;
166	- then add group commit;
167	- then consider `io_uring` with registered journal segments;
168	- only consider `mmap` if benchmarks prove syscall overhead, not storage latency, is the bottleneck.
169	
170	Effort: large, and not first.
171	
172	**Recommended Order**
173	
174	Start with the Committed Response Journal and replay reader, even though it will expose latency cost. Then replace byte-copy rings with owned buffer descriptors. Then remove network and outbox per-message allocation. Then tighten `ResponseSink` so journal commit and ack publication cannot diverge. Finally, add advanced async disk I/O only after the simpler durable path is correct and measured.
175	
176	The key architectural point is that durability and zero-copy should share one design: responses move through owned buffers into journal segments, journal commit publishes ack eligibility, and replay reads the exact same committed byte stream that golden fixtures freeze. That gives `spine` a path to lower latency without losing the ability to prove what happened after a crash.
177
  81. assistantassistant2026-06-27 20:52:20 UTC
    thinking
  82. assistantassistant2026-06-27 20:52:22 UTC
    Codex completed with a strong, well-structured review (it ran read-only and worked from my verified brief rather than re-reading files, but the recommendations are concrete). Key contributions noted: a unified commit-ordering point (`fold → journal append → durable commit → ack publish → TCP write`), three honestly-named ack policies (Strict Durable / Bounded Loss / Replicated Commit), a **Buffer Ownership Ring** (move descriptors, not bytes), a **Journal Segment Ring**, making `ResponseSink` return a commit position so disk and TCP bytes never diverge, and replay-driven golden fixtures with per-byte crash-cut tests.

The two Claude agents are still reading the real code for code-cited depth. Waiting on them.
