<result>I have everything I need. Here is the deliverable.

---

# Architecture recommendation — rust-raw single-sequencer (`spine`)

## Framing: two findings that reshape the brief

Before the ranked list, two facts from the code change what "durability + replay" and "zero-copy" actually mean here:

**The `.obox` is derived output, not a replay source.** The brief asks that the response log "become a committed journal AND a replay/recovery path that reconstructs engine state from the journal." But `.obox` holds *post-fold outcome frames* (fills, status, balances), and the contract itself records that two pieces of authoritative state — the per-market open-interest counter and the dedup-key set — have *no Block-3 Response witness* (`contract/boundary-adapter.md:122`). You therefore cannot rebuild `Sequencer` state by replaying `.obox`. The deterministic source of truth is the **input command stream** (`docs/system-design.md:117-123, 327`), and rust-raw does not durably persist it anywhere: input frames land in the ingress ring (`src/net.rs:220`) and are dropped after the fold. So priority (1) is not "add fsync to `outbox.rs`" — it is "introduce a durable input/sequence log that today does not exist, and make *that* the replay source." The `.obox` is a second, derived durable artifact that must be hardened separately for exactly-once.

**The latency gate is blind to the transport.** `benches/fast_path.rs` drives `accept_with_sink` directly with an in-memory `BenchSink` (`benches/fast_path.rs:283-306, 362`). It never touches the ring, the TCP framing, the ack path, or disk. So the per-command allocations in `ring.rs`, `net.rs`, and the ack path (below) **do not appear in the ~3.8µs/1µs number at all**, and fsync is entirely outside it. This cuts both ways: zero-copy work on the transport will not move the headline gate (so it cannot be validated by it), and adding fsync will not regress the gate either (so the gate cannot protect the durability budget). Every recommendation below names the *additional* honesty instrument needed because the current gate cannot see the change.

---

## Ranked recommendations

### 1. Introduce the durable sequence log as the replay source of truth
**Changes:** A new append-only input journal that captures each sequenced command's raw bytes, plus a startup replay path. This is the precondition for all of priority (1); nothing else delivers recovery.

**Mechanism:** "One binary format end to end" (`docs/system-design.md:60-67`) means the ingress payload *is* the log record — no re-encode. In `run_core`, the raw `item.bytes` popped from the ingress ring (`src/sequencer.rs:307`) is exactly what to frame (`seq`, `crc32c`, parent-chain — see item 3) and append. Recovery on startup: validate the header (`wire::validate_obox_header`), walk frames with `validate_log_frame` (`src/wire/mod.rs:434`), and feed each record body back through `Sequencer::accept` — which rebuilds the matcher, accounts, OI counter, *and* the dedup map (`src/sequencer.rs:193-237`) for free, because dedup already lives in journaled state.

**Effect:** Unlocks crash recovery and replay-equivalence. Durability is bounded by the journaler's fsync policy (item 2), not the fold. No throughput cost on the fold thread if journaling is off-thread (item 2).

**Correctness risk + harness:** Replaying the input log must reproduce byte-identical `.obox`. Guard with a replay-equivalence test that runs each golden input stream, then *replays the captured input log into a fresh process* and asserts the regenerated `.obox` diffs clean against the frozen fixture (`contract/harness/golden/frozen-obox/`). This is the TigerBeetle StateChecker re-aimed at cross-time equivalence (`docs/tigerbeetle-harvest.md:157`).

**Effort:** Medium-Large.

### 2. Re-split the journaler onto its own cursor with group-commit fsync; release acks on the durable cursor
**Changes:** Restore the three-consumer ring topology the spec prescribes (`docs/system-design.md:126-153`) that rust-raw collapsed. Today the core thread folds *and* writes the outbox inline via the `Outbox` sink (`src/sequencer.rs:308`), and the ack is pushed to the ack ring *before* anything is durable (`src/sequencer.rs:313`). That collapse is fine for a flush-only stub but breaks the moment fsync enters: an inline `fdatasync` would land directly on the fold's 1µs budget.

**Mechanism:** A dedicated journaler consumer reads the sequenced stream, appends to the input log (item 1), and issues **one `fdatasync` per group-commit batch** — flush on fill or ~100µs, whichever first (`docs/system-design.md:155-168`, `tigerbeetle-harvest.md:200`). The journaler advances a `durable_cursor`; the SEQUENCED ack is released by the journaler *after* the batch's fsync, not by the fold (`docs/system-design.md:143-148`). The matcher runs ahead in memory; no `.obox` frame escapes past `min(matching_cursor, durable_cursor)`.

**Effect:** This is the resolution of the fsync-vs-1µs tension (expanded below). Fold latency stays compute-bound (~1µs target unaffected); ack latency absorbs the amortized fsync tail; throughput scales with batch size at ~2M cmd/s.

**Correctness risk + harness:** The harness still expects exactly one SEQUENCED per input (`boundary-adapter.md` obligation 3) — now delivered later and in batches, but unchanged in content; goldens stay byte-identical (I/O is below the determinism boundary, `tigerbeetle-harvest.md:200`). New instrument: a crash-injection test biased to the in-flight fsync window (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:159`) asserting no acked command is ever lost and no un-acked command is ever durable.

**Effort:** Large (it is a structural re-split, plus a second cursor and backpressure).

### 3. Per-record hash-chain, trailing-seal framing, and the three-case recovery policy
**Changes:** Make both logs self-verifying and give recovery a precise torn-tail vs interior-corruption decision. The current frame carries a self-CRC over `seq+record` and a self-locating `seq` (`src/wire/mod.rs:423-431`) — good — but **no parent chain**, so a spliced or reordered region is undetectable, and there is no recovery policy at all.

**Mechanism:** Add `parent = checksum(previous_frame)` (genesis 0) to the log frame (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:136`); the chain head at seq N becomes a single compact equivalence witness. Recovery implements three cases (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:139`): clean; torn tail (CRC fails *at* the durable cursor → truncate to last chain-consistent record — nothing past the ack boundary was promised); interior corruption (CRC fails *inside* the fsync'd region → halt and declare loss, never silently truncate). Add the cheap single-node gaps: `fsync(fd)` on startup before any read, and `fsync(dir_fd)` after creating each segment (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:201`).

**Effect:** Turns "the log is truth" into a checkable claim; negligible hot-path cost (one extra checksum per record, already CRC'ing).

**Correctness risk + harness:** Adding the parent field **changes every emitted byte** — this is an intentional reframe of the frozen format, so re-freeze all `frozen-obox/` fixtures *once* and cross-check the recomputed chain head against an independent implementation so the re-freeze isn't laundering a bug (the `.obox-blind rollback` lesson). Add hand-corrupted golden logs for each recovery case.

**Effort:** Medium (chain) + Medium (recovery walk).

### 4. Zero-copy ingress: read TCP straight into the ring slot
**Changes:** Eliminate the three per-command copies on the ingress hop. Today `read_frame_eof` allocates `vec![0u8; len]` then copies again with `payload[1..].to_vec()` (`src/net.rs:308,314`), and `try_push` copies the payload a third time into the slot (`src/ring.rs:101`), and `try_pop` allocates a fresh `Vec` via `slot.bytes[..len].to_vec()` (`src/ring.rs:137`).

**Mechanism:** Two new ring APIs. A fill-in-place producer (`reserve` a slot, hand `&amp;mut slot.bytes` to the reader so `read_exact` lands the frame body directly in the slot, then publish with the true length) collapses all three copies into one TCP read. A borrowing consumer (a read-guard derefing to `&amp;[u8]` that advances `head` on drop, or a `consume(|bytes, ingress_ns|)` closure) removes the `to_vec`. `accept_with_sink` already takes `&amp;[u8]` (`src/sequencer.rs:173`) and decoding is already zero-copy through the borrowing `Reader` (`src/wire/mod.rs:46-72`), so the fold composes unchanged.

**Effect:** The single largest hot-path allocation win; restores the "zero allocation on the hot path" invariant (`docs/system-design.md:54-57`) on ingress. Caps record size at `SLOT_CAP=4096` (`src/ring.rs:7`) — a documented Capacity-Budget bound (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:177`), already enforced via `RingError::TooLarge` (`src/net.rs:222`).

**Correctness risk + harness:** Must not perturb a single emitted byte — so the frozen `.obox` diff *is* the guard (a corrupted in-place read fails at the first bad `seq`). The gate bench can't see this path, so add (a) a property test feeding random frames through both the in-place ring and the old copy ring asserting identical bytes, and (b) an end-to-end alloc gate using the existing `alloc_counter` (`src/alloc_counter.rs:59`) asserting zero steady-state allocations across net→ring→fold, since the latency gate won't.

**Effort:** Medium.

### 5. Zero-copy and batched egress: ack path and TCP writes
**Changes:** The ack hop mirrors ingress waste — `encode_ack_record` allocates a fresh `Vec` per ack (`src/sequencer.rs:332`), pushed (copy) into the ack ring, popped (`to_vec`), then `writer_loop` builds *another* fresh `Vec` per frame and does `write_all` + `flush` **per message** — a syscall per ack with no `TCP_NODELAY` set anywhere (grep confirms none) (`src/net.rs:257-261`).

**Mechanism:** Encode the ack into a reusable scratch buffer (or directly into the ack-ring slot via the item-4 producer API); on the writer, set `TCP_NODELAY` once, coalesce ready acks and emit with a single `writev`/batched `write`, and flush once per batch rather than per frame. The 50µs sleep-poll (`src/net.rs:271`) stays as the light-load backstop.

**Effect:** Removes ~4 allocations and one syscall per command on egress; batched writes are the throughput lever for the ack stream under load.

**Correctness risk + harness:** Ack *content* and per-input correlation by `Idem` are unchanged (`boundary-adapter.md` obligation 3); batching only changes timing. Same end-to-end alloc gate from item 4 covers it. `TCP_NODELAY` changes wire *timing*, never bytes — goldens unaffected.

**Effort:** Medium.

### 6. Zero-copy outbox encode
**Changes:** Each `append_response_frame`/`append_response_record` allocates a fresh `Vec` per frame before handing it to the `BufWriter` (`src/outbox.rs:26-28,38-42`).

**Mechanism:** Encode the log frame directly into a reusable scratch buffer owned by `Outbox` (or directly into the `BufWriter`'s buffer), reset per frame. `encode_log_frame_record` already takes a closure writing into a caller buffer (`src/wire/mod.rs:415`), so this is a small plumbing change.

**Effect:** Removes N allocations per command (one per emitted response) on the publisher path; small but on the hot path in the real net pipeline.

**Correctness risk + harness:** Pure refactor of byte production — frozen `.obox` diff catches any divergence directly.

**Effort:** Small.

### 7. (Lower priority, optional) O_DIRECT + sector-aligned batch buffers; snapshot with atomic-flip manifest
**Changes:** O_DIRECT page-cache bypass paired with `fdatasync`/FUA per batch (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:199`), removing the failed-fsync-serves-dirty-pages hazard; and a checksummed snapshot with atomic manifest promotion (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:214-217`) to bound restart time (`docs/system-design.md:463-478`).

**Mechanism:** Sector-aligned (4096) pre-allocated batch buffers (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:203`); snapshot via a dedicated replay consumer or COW fork, write-fresh→fsync→verify→flip, prune log only after the new snapshot is durable.

**Effect:** O_DIRECT hardens the durability *failure mode* (not latency); snapshots cut replay tail (~2 min at 5-min interval, `docs/system-design.md:472`). Both are follow-ons, not blockers.

**Correctness risk + harness:** O_DIRECT alignment must pad the partial tail sector — a decision that affects on-disk bytes (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:203`); pin it and re-freeze. Snapshot load must verify checksum and fall back to log-from-zero on mismatch (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:215`) or it resurrects wrong state.

**Effort:** Large each.

---

## Where fsync durability and the ~1µs budget pull against each other — and the resolution

The collision is only apparent, and it exists today *because* rust-raw collapsed the journaler into the fold thread (`src/sequencer.rs:308`). A single NVMe `fdatasync` is ~10µs–1ms; calling it per command, inline, would blow the 1µs fold budget by one to three orders of magnitude, and at 2M cmd/s it is simply impossible.

The resolution is structural, and the spec already names it: **the fold budget and the durability seam live on different cursors and different threads** (`docs/system-design.md:143-168`). The 1µs the gate measures is *compute-path* latency — emit minus ingress in the SUT clock domain (`boundary-adapter.md:154-159`) — and the fold never calls fsync. The fsync tail lives in *ack* latency, the durable-cursor round-trip, which the contract measures as a separate number. Item 2 makes this real: the journaler batches and issues one `fdatasync` per group-commit window (~100µs), so fsync cost is amortized across the whole batch and the per-command durable cost trends toward (fsync tail ÷ batch size) — at 2M cmd/s a 100µs window batches ~200 commands per sync. The matcher runs ahead in memory; acks are released by the journaler post-sync. Group commit is the lever that lets durability and the compute budget coexist *because they were never the same measurement* — they only collide if you keep journaling inline.

Two honesty caveats fall out of this. First, the current gate cannot police the durability budget (it never fsyncs), so item 2 must ship with a *separate* ack-latency/durable-cursor benchmark and the fsync-window crash-injection test (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:159`) — otherwise group-commit regressions are invisible. Second, the interior-corruption policy (item 3) is the one place durability scope and the ack contract genuinely conflict: auto-truncating past corruption would silently drop acked commands (`tigerbeetle-harvest.md:139`, open question `:348`). The spec's answer — and the correct one — is halt-and-declare-loss for interior corruption, truncate only the torn tail beyond the durable cursor, because everything inside the fsync'd region was promised to a client.</result>

  86. assistantassistant2026-06-27 20:53:33 UTC
    Claude agent #1 returned an exceptional, code-grounded review that **corrects the brief on two pivotal points** — both verified against the contract docs:

1. **`.obox` is derived output, not a replay source.** It holds post-fold outcomes; the per-market open-interest counter and the dedup-key set have *no response witness* (`boundary-adapter.md:122`), so you cannot rebuild engine state from it. The true replay source is the **durable input command stream** — which rust-raw persists *nowhere* today (input frames are dropped after the fold). So priority (1) is "introduce a durable input log and make that the replay source," not "fsync the outbox."
2. **The latency gate is blind to the transport.** `benches/fast_path.rs` drives `accept_with_sink` with an in-memory sink — it never touches the ring, TCP, ack path, or disk. So zero-copy transport work *won't move the headline number* (can't be validated by it) and fsync *won't regress it* (can't be protected by it). Every change needs its own honesty instrument — the `alloc_counter` for zero-copy, a separate ack/durable-cursor latency bench + crash-injection for durability.

It also resolves the fsync-vs-1µs tension structurally (separate cursors/threads, group-commit; the fold gate is compute-only, fsync lives in ack latency) and adds a per-record hash-chain + 3-case recovery policy.

Waiting on Claude agent #2 (the deep journal + zero-copy design) before I synthesize.

<result>I have everything I need. Here is the deliverable.

---

# Deep design: durable committed journal + zero-copy path for the rust-raw spine

## Foundation finding that drives both halves

The spine's outputs are a **pure deterministic function of its sequenced input stream**. The only clock the engine reads is `self.now`, and that is set exclusively from `ClockTickRequest.now` — a sequenced input record (`sequencer.rs:1007-1008`), and every `emitted_at` stamp in an outbox record is copied from it (`sequencer.rs:1155`, `sequencer.rs:2543`). No `now_mono_ns()` enters the fold. Therefore: **replaying the input command stream regenerates the engine state *and* the `.obox` output bytes exactly.** This single fact is the spine of the whole design — it tells us *what to journal* (inputs, not outputs) and *how to verify recovery* (replay must reproduce the frozen golden `.obox` byte-for-byte).

---

## Part A — Durable committed journal with crash-safe replay

### What must be journaled: the Sequenced Command Journal

Today nothing on the input side is persisted. Records flow TCP → ingress ring → `run_core` fold and are discarded (`net.rs:220`, `sequencer.rs:307-308`); the only file written is the `.obox`, which holds **outputs** (`Ack`/`Fill`/`OrderResponse` …) via `Outbox::append_response_*` (`outbox.rs:25-42`). Outputs are the wrong thing to make authoritative: a resting order that never fills, an id-generator counter, a dedup key — none of these are reconstructible from the response stream alone. Reverse-engineering state from outputs is lossy and fragile.

The committed journal must therefore be the **sequenced input command log**: for each record admitted past ingress framing, in arrival order, one frame of `[len][seq][crc][parent_crc][input record bytes]`. Inputs only. The fold regenerates everything else, including the `.obox`, which becomes a **derived projection** — still emitted (it is the external contract and the golden oracle), but fully reconstructible, never the source of truth.

This cleanly answers *inputs vs outputs vs snapshots*:

- **Journal (always):** the sequenced input records. Minimal and complete.
- **Snapshot (periodically):** the full recovered-state set, to bound replay — see below.
- **Never journal for recovery:** outputs, and the transient `MatcherRollback` (`matcher.rs:429`). Rollback snapshots live and die inside one `fold` call and never escape a command, so they are not recovered state.

The recovered-state set a snapshot must capture is exactly the live `Sequencer` plus `Engine`: `next_seq`; the dedup map and **both** dedup arenas (`dedup`, `dedup_response_bytes`, `dedup_response_frames` — `sequencer.rs:122-130`), because a post-snapshot redelivery must replay the original stored frames; the accounts (collateral, positions, leverage, status); the book (resting orders per market); the id generators (`me_seq`, venue order id); `now`; and market/collateral/fee config. Conveniently `Sequencer` already derives `Clone` (`sequencer.rs:132-154`), so a consistent cut for an off-thread snapshot is already feasible without a process fork.

### The fsync boundary relative to the client ack

The durability contract is **commit-before-ack**: the input record at sequence N must be on stable storage before the `Ack` for N leaves the socket. Concretely the `Ack` carries `seq` already, and the ack hop's `RingItem.ingress_ns` field is a dead `0` today (`sequencer.rs:314`) — repurpose it to carry the gating seq. The pipeline becomes:

```
reader → ingress ring → compute(run_core):
    pop input (borrowed) → assign_seq → append (seq, bytes) to journal staging
    → fold → push ack tagged with seq to ack_ring
journaler thread: owns the fd; fdatasyncs batches; publishes durable_cursor (atomic)
writer thread: releases an ack only once durable_cursor &gt;= ack.seq
```

The writer gating against an atomic `durable_cursor` is the entire commit-before-ack mechanism, and it is a small change because the ack already knows its seq.

### Where fsync latency fights the ~1µs budget, and how it is resolved

An `fdatasync` on NVMe is ~10–100µs typical, with a tail into milliseconds. The compute thread runs ~1µs/order. Issuing an fsync per order on the compute path would inflate the budget by one to two orders of magnitude and destroy the latency gate. The resolution is structural, in layers:

1. **fsync is off the compute thread.** A dedicated **journaler/committer thread** owns the fd and the `fdatasync`. The compute thread only `memcpy`s the input bytes into a staging/mmap region and bumps a write cursor — **zero syscalls on the hot path.** The 1µs gate is measured with amortized batch timing on the compute path (per the perf context), and that path stays syscall-free, so the *gate metric is unaffected by durability*. The fsync cost surfaces only in end-to-end ack latency, which is a different measurement — state this explicitly so the team does not conflate the compute-gate number with the durability latency.

2. **Group commit.** One `fdatasync` per **group-commit batch**, flushed on batch-fill or a ~50–100µs timer. At ~2M cmd/s a 100µs window batches ~200 commands per fsync, amortizing a 50µs fsync to ~0.25µs of throughput cost per order — and that cost is borne by the journaler, not the sequencer. This is exactly the tigerbeetle-harvest §8 verdict: batch-`fdatasync`, one durable-cursor advance per fsync.

3. **The Durability Posture is an explicit knob, not a hidden default.** Commit-before-ack adds up to one flush-interval of *ack latency* (never compute time). If the p99 ack gate cannot absorb ~100µs, the alternative posture is **async-commit with a bounded-loss window**: release acks immediately, fsync lazily, and on crash lose only the un-fsync'd tail (≤ one batch). This trades the strict contract for latency and must be named and chosen deliberately — TigerBeetle's harvest is emphatic that you never tell a client "durable" when it was not, so async-commit must be labelled as accept-bounded-loss, not silently adopted.

To shave the fsync tail further: `O_DIRECT` + `RWF_DSYNC`/FUA writes via io_uring, sector-aligned (4096) batch buffers, plus `fsync(dir_fd)` once after the journal segment is created (the classic directory-entry gotcha). `O_DIRECT` also removes the post-failed-fsync dirty-page hazard (a failed fsync can leave the kernel serving stale dirty pages with no error). Any fsync error is fatal — never advance `durable_cursor`, never ack.

### Crash-consistency of the on-disk format

The frame format is already most of the way there. `encode_log_frame_record` lays out `[u32 frame_length][u64 seq][u32 crc32c][record]`, where the CRC covers `seq ‖ record` (`wire/mod.rs:411-432`), and `validate_log_frame` requires `frame_length == remaining` and a CRC match (`wire/mod.rs:434-452`). The 64-byte `.obox` header carries its own CRC and a `base_seq` (`wire/mod.rs:454-490`). Three gaps to close for a true committed journal:

- **No hash-chain.** The seq is self-locating but frames are not linked. Add `parent = crc(previous frame)` (genesis 0) per tigerbeetle-harvest §5. This turns the log into a self-verifying list — gaps, reorders, dup-splices all become detectable — and gives a one-value equivalence witness (the chain head at N) instead of diffing whole logs.
- **Torn-tail vs interior must be distinguished.** A torn final write (power loss mid-batch) shows up as a length-prefix `ShortRead` or a CRC mismatch **at the tail**, i.e. at/after the durable cursor — nothing past the ack boundary was ever promised, so **truncate to the last chain-consistent frame**. A CRC mismatch **inside** the fsync'd region is interior corruption of acked data → **halt and declare data loss**, never silently truncate (the FAST'18 lesson). The durable cursor is what separates the two cases.
- **CRC position.** The CRC sits before the record, not trailing; with the length prefix + CRC-over-(seq‖record) this still detects a torn record (mismatch), but adopting the trailing-seal discipline (validating bytes written last) makes "complete vs torn final write" unambiguous for variable-length frames.

### Recovery procedure and its byte-oracle

On restart: `fsync(fd)` to drain a crashed predecessor's in-flight writes; load the latest snapshot whose manifest checksum verifies (else fall back to the previous snapshot, ultimately to genesis); seed the `Sequencer`/`Engine` from it; then **replay the input journal from `snapshot.seq + 1`** through the *unmodified* `accept_with_sink` (`sequencer.rs:173`), asserting each frame's `seq == expected_next` and chain-parent matches. Replay rebuilds the book, accounts, id generators, and — because every admitted record (including malformed sheds and redeliveries) is journaled in arrival order — the **dedup arenas rebuild identically**, so a redelivery in the tail re-hits the rebuilt dedup entry and replays the same stored frames (`sequencer.rs:194-199`). Malformed records reproduce the same `shed_malformed_ack` because the decision is deterministic from the bytes (`sequencer.rs:184-186`).

The verification that recovered state byte-equals a clean run is **free**, because outputs are deterministic: feed the recovery a scenario's input journal and assert the regenerated `.obox` is byte-identical to the corresponding frozen golden under `contract/harness/golden/frozen-obox/`. That is precisely what the existing header-skipped frame-diff runner already does. Add one crash-recovery test that kills the process mid-stream, restarts, replays, and diffs against the golden — and a torn-tail test that truncates the journal mid-frame and asserts recovery truncates to the last good frame and still matches the golden up to the durable cursor. The reject-then-continue and deposit-redelivery goldens specifically exercise the rebuilt-dedup and rebuilt-rollback paths, so they are the load-bearing recovery fixtures.

### Snapshots and log truncation

Replay time is bounded by journal length since genesis, so snapshot every S sequences (or T bytes). Discipline from tigerbeetle-harvest §8: take the consistent cut via `Sequencer::clone()` handed to the snapshot thread (no fork; the hot path keeps sequencing); write the blob fresh; `fdatasync`; checksum-verify on readback; **atomically flip a small fixed-layout manifest** holding `(sequence S, blob location, blob checksum)`; keep the previous snapshot until the new one is durable+verified; only **then truncate the input journal behind S**. A crash mid-snapshot leaves the previous snapshot fully selectable. Loading a corrupt snapshot must fall back, never resurrect wrong deterministic state.

---

## Part B — Zero-copy across network → ring → engine → disk

### The copies and allocations today, counted per message

Ingress: `read_frame_eof` does `vec![0u8; frame_length]` (`net.rs:308`) then `payload[1..].to_vec()` to strip the kind byte (`net.rs:314`) — 2 allocs; `ingress.push` copies into the slot (`ring.rs:101`); `try_pop` does `slot.bytes[..len].to_vec()` (`ring.rs:137`) — 1 alloc + copy. Egress: `encode_ack_record` allocates a fresh `Vec` (`sequencer.rs:332-335`); `ack_ring.push` copies into the slot; `try_pop` `to_vec`s out; `writer_loop` builds a fresh `Vec` per frame and does `write_all` **+ `flush` per message** — a syscall per message, no `TCP_NODELAY`, no batching (`net.rs:257-261`). Outbox: each `append_response_frame` allocates a fresh `Vec` then copies into the `BufWriter` (`outbox.rs:27,44-49`). Round-trip: **~7–9 heap allocations, ~8 copies, 2 socket syscalls per message.** Target: ~0 steady-state allocs.

### Read-Into-Slot ring (collapse socket → ring copy)

Give the SPSC producer a `reserve() -&gt; &amp;mut Slot` / `commit(len, ingress_ns)` pair so the reader does `stream.read_exact(&amp;mut slot.bytes[..len])` **directly into the next free ring slot**, then publishes `tail`. The kind byte is consumed by offset, not by `to_vec`. **Soundness:** SPSC means the producer solely owns the reserved slot until `commit` publishes `tail`; the consumer provably cannot observe it earlier (`ring.rs:91-104`). **Lifetime:** `reserve` returns a `&amp;mut` bound to the producer's borrow; `commit` consumes it, so only one reservation is open at a time. This removes both ingress socket allocs (`net.rs:308,314`) and the producer copy (`ring.rs:101`): ingress drops from 2 allocs + 2 copies to **one kernel→slot copy, zero allocs**.

### Borrowed-Slot pop (lend, don't copy out)

Replace `try_pop`'s `to_vec` (`ring.rs:137`) with a `PopGuard` that borrows `&amp;slot.bytes[..len]` and advances `head` on `Drop`. The fold already takes `&amp;[u8]` (`accept_with_sink(&amp;item.bytes, …)`, `sequencer.rs:308`), so it consumes the borrow directly. **Soundness:** SPSC means the producer cannot overwrite the slot at `head` until the consumer advances `head` — exactly what the guard defers to `Drop` — so the borrow is valid for the guard's lifetime; the consumer processes one item fully before popping the next. This removes the ingress-pop alloc+copy and, applied symmetrically, the ack-pop alloc+copy (`ring.rs:137` on both rings).

### Encode-In-Place Outbox (single buffer = in-memory response + durable record)

mmap the `.obox` region and make the `ResponseSink` encode response bytes **directly at the mmap tail cursor** instead of into a fresh `Vec` + `BufWriter` (`outbox.rs:27,44-49`). The `ArenaResponseSink` already encodes once into the dedup byte arena and hands `&amp;self.byte_arena[start..end]` onward (`sequencer.rs:425-431`); redirect the durable write to the same encode. One encode produces (a) the in-memory bytes the writer reads to build the TCP frame and (b) the durable record after `msync`/`fdatasync`. This removes the per-frame outbox alloc and the BufWriter copy. The same single-buffer technique applies to the **input** journal: the one remaining ingress copy (borrowed slot → mmap'd journal staging at the write cursor) *is* the durability write — a memcpy into mapped pages, not a syscall — then group-fsync'd by the journaler.

### Batched, vectored egress (kill the per-message syscall)

Set `TCP_NODELAY` once at accept. In `writer_loop`, drain all currently-available acks and emit them with a single `writev`/`io_uring` submit per wakeup, instead of `write_all` + `flush` per ack (`net.rs:257-261`). Encode each frame in place (or gather slot borrows into an `iovec` array) so the vectored write sends N acks in one syscall. For the dedup-redelivery replay path specifically, `splice`/`sendfile` from the mmap'd `.obox` straight to the socket is available, since those bytes already exist on disk.

### io_uring on both seams

A shared io_uring (or one per thread) batches socket reads, socket writes, and the journal `write`+`fdatasync` as one submission per tick — the journaler submits the group-commit batch + barrier fsync in a single enter. This is the natural home for the off-thread, batched durability described in Part A, and keeps the compute thread free of syscalls entirely.

### Net effect

Per message the path goes from **~8 allocs + ~8 copies + 2 socket syscalls** to **~0 steady-state allocs, ~2 copies (kernel→slot in, encoded-buffer→socket out), and amortized syscalls** (one `writev` per writer wakeup, one `fdatasync` per ~200-command batch). The one structural copy that remains — borrowed slot → mmap'd journal — is load-bearing: it is the act of making the bytes durable, and it doubles as the in-memory response under Encode-In-Place. The dedup arena copy (`sequencer.rs:420,427`) also stays, because redelivery needs an owned, truncation-stable copy independent of the mmap'd output region.

### Correctness guards (ti

  107. assistantassistant2026-06-27 20:56:09 UTC
    Three independent reviews are in — **Codex** (whole-architecture) and **two Claude agents** (one whole-architecture, one deep journal+zero-copy design). They converged hard, and the two Claude agents (which read the actual code and contract docs) corrected the brief on two points I've now verified myself. Here's the synthesis.

---

## The headline: two facts reshape the brief before any work starts

**1. The `.obox` log is *derived output*, not a replay source.** It stores post-fold outcomes (Ack/Fill/balances). But the per-market open-interest counter and the dedup-key set have **no response witness** (`contract/boundary-adapter.md:122`) — you cannot rebuild engine state from `.obox`. The fold is **deterministic from the sequenced input stream**: the only clock it reads is `self.now`, set solely from `ClockTickRequest.now` (`sequencer.rs:1008`); `now_mono_ns()` appears only in telemetry outside the fold (`sequencer.rs:310`, verified). So:

> "Add durability + replay to the logs" means **introduce a durable Sequenced Command Journal of inputs** — which rust-raw persists *nowhere* today (ingress frames are dropped after the fold) — and replay *that* through the unmodified `accept_with_sink`. The `.obox` stays a derived projection, hardened separately. Because the fold is deterministic, the frozen golden `.obox` becomes a **free recovery oracle**: replay must reproduce it byte-for-byte.

**2. The latency gate is blind to the transport.** `benches/fast_path.rs` drives `accept_with_sink` against an in-memory sink — it never touches the ring, TCP, ack path, or disk. So the ~3.8µs→1µs number is *compute-path only*. **Zero-copy transport work won't move it** (so the gate can't validate those wins — use `src/alloc_counter.rs` as the mechanical zero-allocation gate), and **fsync won't regress it** (so the gate can't protect the durability budget — that needs a *separate* durable-cursor/ack-latency benchmark + crash-injection test). Every change below ships with its own honesty instrument.

## The unifying idea: durability and zero-copy are one design

All three reviewers independently landed here: **bytes move through owned buffers into journal segments; journal commit gates ack eligibility; replay reads the exact committed byte stream the goldens freeze.** One buffer is simultaneously the in-memory message and the durable record. Don't build two separate subsystems.

## Recommended roadmap (ranked)

**Pure-performance, independently shippable, no format change:**

1. **Read-Into-Slot + Borrowed-Slot ring.** Give the SPSC producer `reserve()/commit()` so the reader does `read_exact` straight into the next free ring slot (kills both `net.rs:308,314` allocs and the `ring.rs:101` copy); replace `try_pop`'s `to_vec` (`ring.rs:137`) with a `PopGuard` that lends `&[u8]` and advances `head` on `Drop`. The fold already takes `&[u8]`, so it composes unchanged. **The SPSC single-producer/single-consumer split is what makes the borrow sound** — the producer provably can't touch a slot until the consumer advances `head`. Round-trip drops from ~8 allocs + ~8 copies + 2 syscalls toward **~0 steady-state allocs**. Guarded by existing ring FIFO/wrap tests (extend to the new APIs, ideally loom/miri) + the frozen goldens + the alloc counter.

2. **Batched vectored egress.** Set `TCP_NODELAY` once at accept; in `writer_loop` drain all ready acks and emit with one `writev` per wakeup instead of `write_all`+`flush` *per message* (`net.rs:257-261`). Removes the per-ack syscall; this one *is* latency-visible.

**Durability core (builds on the determinism property):**

3. **Sequenced Command Journal + commit-before-ack, off-thread.** Re-split the journaler onto its own cursor (the spec's 3-consumer topology that rust-raw collapsed into the fold at `sequencer.rs:308`). The fold only memcpys input bytes into an mmap'd staging region and bumps a cursor — **zero syscalls on the hot path**. A dedicated journaler owns the fd, issues **one `fdatasync` per group-commit batch** (~100µs window ≈ 200 commands/sync at 2M cmd/s), and advances an atomic `durable_cursor`. The writer releases an ack only once `durable_cursor >= ack.seq` — and the ack already carries `seq`, so the gating is cheap (repurpose the dead `RingItem.ingress_ns=0` on the ack hop).

4. **Self-verifying frames + recovery policy.** The format is already most of the way there — per-frame `crc32c(seq‖record)` and a CRC'd header with `base_seq` (`wire/mod.rs:411-490`). Add `parent = crc(previous frame)` to make the log a hash-chain (gaps/reorders/splices become detectable; the chain head is a one-value equivalence witness). Recovery has **three cases**: clean; **torn tail** (CRC fails *at/after* the durable cursor → truncate to last chain-consistent frame, nothing past the ack boundary was promised); **interior corruption** (CRC fails *inside* the fsync'd region → **halt and declare loss, never silently truncate**). Plus the cheap single-node gaps: `fsync(fd)` on startup, `fsync(dir_fd)` after segment creation. Adding `parent` changes every emitted byte — re-freeze all goldens **once**, cross-checking the chain head against an independent computation so the re-freeze can't launder a bug (the `.obox`-blind-rollback lesson).

**Follow-ons:**

5. **Encode-In-Place outbox over mmap + io_uring on both seams.** The `ResponseSink` encodes responses directly at the mmap tail (`ArenaResponseSink` already encodes once and lends `&byte_arena[start..end]`, `sequencer.rs:425-431`) — one encode yields both the bytes the writer frames *and* the durable record after `msync`. A shared io_uring batches socket reads/writes and the journal `write`+`fdatasync` per tick. `splice`/`sendfile` from the mmap'd `.obox` to the socket for the redelivery-replay path.

6. **Snapshot manifest + atomic flip + journal truncation** to bound replay time. `Sequencer` already derives `Clone` (`sequencer.rs:132`), so an off-thread consistent cut is feasible without a fork: write fresh → fdatasync → checksum-verify → atomically flip a fixed-layout manifest → only then truncate the journal behind the snapshot. Corrupt snapshot must fall back, never resurrect wrong state.

## The fsync-vs-1µs tension — resolved, not finessed

It's only an *apparent* conflict, and it exists today *because* rust-raw collapsed the journaler into the fold thread. An NVMe `fdatasync` is ~10–100µs (tail into ms); per-command inline fsync would blow the budget by 1–2 orders of magnitude. The resolution is structural: **the compute budget and the durability seam are different measurements on different threads.** The 1µs gate is compute-path latency (fold never fsyncs); the fsync tail lives in *ack* latency (the durable-cursor round-trip), a separate number. Group commit amortizes one fsync across ~200 commands. The **Durability Posture must be an explicit, named knob**: *Strict Durable Acknowledgement* (ack waits for group commit — correct, higher ack tail) vs *Bounded-Loss Acknowledgement* (ack before fsync, documented ≤1-batch loss window). Never tell a client "durable" when it wasn't — pick the posture deliberately, ship Strict first as the correct baseline.

## Where the three reviewers differed

Very little — they converged on the unified buffer-ownership model, group-commit, the hash-chain, and the recovery policy independently. The only material gap: **Codex ran read-only and worked from my brief rather than re-reading files**, so it missed both reframes above (it assumed `.obox` was the replay source). The two code-reading Claude agents caught and corrected that, and I verified the determinism property myself. Codex's distinct contributions that survive: naming the Journal Segment Ring, making `ResponseSink` return a commit position so disk and TCP bytes can't diverge, and the "benchmark modes that don't lie" framing (report *sequenced* vs *durably-acked* latency as two numbers).

---

The full agent write-ups (with more file:line detail and the per-message copy/alloc accounting) are in my scratchpad if you want them verbatim. **Want me to (a) commit this as a design doc under `experiments/single-sequencer/docs/`** following the repo's named-term conventions, **(b) go deeper on any single item** (the ring `reserve/commit` + `PopGuard` API is the highest-leverage first step and is fully self-contained), or **(c) spin up a Codex implementation pass** on step 1 behind the alloc-counter gate?

1:# Trading Exchange — System Design Guidelines
3:## 1. Overview
34:## 2. Core Principles
71:## 3. Ingress & Sequencing
73:### Inputs
81:### Sequencing
90:### Backpressure → explicit shed at the boundary
108:### Time is a sequenced input event

126:## 4. Ring Buffer & Consumers
138:### Two things flow back to the client — keep them separate
155:### Batching (design decision)

306:## 5. Reliability & Durability Goals

  116. assistantassistant2026-06-27 21:02:52 UTC

  117. assistantassistant2026-06-27 21:02:53 UTC

    {
  "file_path": "/home/grace/.herdr/worktrees/synfinex/single-sequencer-consolidated/experiments/single-sequencer/docs/system-design.md"

  118. useruser2026-06-27 21:02:53 UTC
    tool result (truncated)
    1	# Trading Exchange — System Design Guidelines
2	
3	## 1. Overview
4	
5	The exchange is built around a **single global sequencer** that imposes a total
6	order on every event entering the system. Once an event is assigned a sequence
7	number and durably logged, the rest of the system is a **deterministic state
8	machine**: feed the same log into a fresh process and you reproduce the exact
9	same state, every time.
10	
11	This is the LMAX Disruptor pattern applied to a multi-asset exchange. The core
12	reads events off an **input queue**, the sequencer stamps each with a sequence
13	number, and the result runs down a ring of consumers:
14	
15	```
16	   input            ┌──────────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐
17	   queue    ──────> │   SEQUENCE LOG   │ ───> │ RING BUFFER  │
18	                    │  (durable, total │      └──────┬───────┘
19	                    │      order)      │             │
20	                    └──────────────────┘   ┌─────────┼──────────────┐
21	                                           v         v              v
22	                                     [ JOURNALER ][ MATCHING ][ PUBLISHER ]
23	                                     fsync log    order books  fsync outbox
24	```
25	
26	The sequencer is the boundary: above it, ordering is a scheduling decision; below
27	it, ordering is **law** — everything downstream of the sequence number is a
28	deterministic function of the log. The core is **agnostic to its transport**: an
29	input event is just bytes that get acked once durably recorded, and outputs land
30	in a local **outbox** an external relay drains.
31	
32	---
33	
34	## 2. Core Principles
35	
36	1. **Single writer.** Exactly one thread/process assigns sequence numbers and
37	   advances the authoritative state. No locks on the hot path, no contention,
38	   no distributed consensus in the critical loop.
39	   - **One global sequencer, not one per market — deliberately.** All 1000 order
40	     books share a *single* consistency domain. This is a firm decision, not a
41	     limitation we tolerate: accounts hold **shared, cross-market collateral**, so
42	     margin and liquidation are inherently cross-market and cannot be sharded
43	     without distributed transactions across shards. One sequencer keeps every
44	     balance/margin decision atomic and serializable for free. We buy that
45	     correctness by making the single thread fast (zero-alloc, mechanical
46	     sympathy, parallelized fan-out) rather than by sharding.
47	2. **Determinism below the sequencer.** Given the sequence log, state is a pure
48	   function of the log. No wall-clock reads, no RNG, no map iteration order, no
49	   floating-point non-determinism in business logic on the hot path.
50	3. **The log is the source of truth.** State in memory is a cache of "the log
51	   replayed up to sequence N." Snapshots are an optimization, never the truth.
52	4. **Mechanical sympathy.** Pre-allocated ring buffer, cache-line-aware layout,
53	   batching at I/O boundaries, no allocation on the hot path.
54	5. **Zero allocation on the hot path.** Steady-state operation allocates nothing.
55	   All buffers (ring buffer slots, command structs, output records) are
56	   pre-allocated and reused. No GC pressure, no page faults from fresh
57	   allocations, no heap fragmentation — this is what protects the p99.99 tail.
58	6. **One binary format, end to end.** The wire format the API sends is the same
59	   binary layout we write to the log and read back on replay. A command is
60	   appended to the log as-is — no parse → re-encode step at the sequencer. This
61	   gives us zero-copy ingress→log and **avoids write amplification**: bytes are
62	   written to durable storage once, in the form they arrived. Each record begins
63	   with a **`(version, type)` header**; replay and the API both dispatch on it, so
64	   the format can evolve (new fields, new command types) without breaking replay
65	   of historical logs — old records keep replaying under their original version.
66	   We never reinterpret old bytes; we add new versions. This is what keeps "same
67	   format forever" and "evolve the API" from being in conflict.
68	
69	---
70	
71	## 3. Ingress & Sequencing
72	
73	### Inputs
74	Input **events** carry the whole vocabulary — orders/cancels/modifies, price and
75	oracle updates, deposit/withdrawal events, clock ticks, config feeds. They arrive
76	on an **input queue**, and the core treats them as **bytes on a source**: it is
77	**agnostic to the transport**, whatever carries an event (a socket, a queue, a
78	replay harness) sits outside the deterministic boundary, and an event is **acked
79	once it is durably recorded** in the log.
80	
81	### Sequencing
82	The sequencer reads the input queue and stamps each event with a monotonic
83	**sequence number**. That number *is* the total order: once an event is assigned
84	sequence number `N`, its position is frozen forever. Which event is read next is a
85	live scheduling choice and need not be deterministic.
86	
87	> **Key invariant:** non-determinism is allowed *up to* the moment of
88	> sequencing, and forbidden *after* it.
89	
90	### Backpressure → explicit shed at the boundary
91	A command is **shed at the boundary, before the log** — rather than blocking the
92	sequencer or silently dropping — on either of two triggers: an input source (or the
93	ring buffer) is **full**, or a command has **waited past its latency budget (Δt)**.
94	Shedding on age as well as on capacity drops an over-budget command *before* the
95	queue physically fills, which protects the tail latency. Backpressure stays
96	*outside* the deterministic core — the hot path never blocks on a slow producer or
97	consumer.
98	
99	The shed must be an **explicit** signal: the caller is told, definitively, that
100	the command **did not enter the log** and may therefore retry safely. The
101	dangerous case is an **unknown** outcome — the caller never received an ack. An
102	unknown is **not** a "try again": a blind retry could double-apply a command that
103	was in fact sequenced. The caller resolves an unknown only by retrying **with its
104	idempotency key**, so the sequencer's dedup filter (§5) collapses the duplicate to
105	the original outcome. Every command therefore carries an idempotency key, and the
106	shed-vs-unknown distinction is part of the client contract.
107	
108	### Time is a sequenced input event
109	Business logic never reads a wall clock. **Clock-tick events** are sequenced
110	inputs like any other, so "current time" is just another ordered event and replay
111	stays deterministic. Time-dependent behaviour depends on them: **order
112	cancellation/expiry (GTD, good-till-date)**, time-in-force (IOC/FOK/GTC),
113	funding/settlement windows, and any rate-limited or scheduled action. Tick
114	granularity is a tunable (e.g. 1 ms); finer ticks cost log volume, coarser ticks
115	coarsen expiry.
116	
117	### The sequence log
118	- Append-only, monotonic sequence numbers.
119	- Each entry is the fully-resolved input command (self-contained — no external
120	  lookups needed at replay time).
121	- This log is what gets journaled to durable storage and what a recovering
122	  process replays on restart.
123	
124	---
125	
126	## 4. Ring Buffer & Consumers
127	
128	Sequenced commands are published into a **pre-allocated ring buffer** (power-of-two
129	size, single producer = the sequencer). Multiple consumers read from the buffer
130	in parallel, each tracking its own cursor:
131	
132	| Consumer       | Responsibility                                              |
133	|----------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|
134	| **Journaler**  | Append to the durable log, `fsync`, advance the durable cursor. |
135	| **Matching**   | Apply the command to the in-memory order books / state.     |
136	| **Publisher**  | Write post-fold outcome events (fills, status, balance changes) to the **fsync outbox**. |
137	
138	### Two things flow back to the client — keep them separate
139	The **ack** and the **outcome** are different events on different cursors, and the
140	design depends on not conflating them:
141	
142	- The **ack** is produced the moment the **journaler** durably persists `N`. It
143	  says only that the command is **sequenced** (in the log) — never that it filled,
144	  passed margin, or matched. It rides the **durable cursor**.
145	- The **outcome events** (fills, order/position status, balance changes) come out
146	  of the **fold** — matching plus its effects — and are written to the **outbox**
147	  *after* `N` is durable, so we never surface an effect we would lose on crash
148	  (§5). They ride `min(matching_cursor, durable_cursor)`.
149	
150	The matching engine may run ahead of the journaler in memory, but no outbox write
151	escapes ahead of the durable cursor. (This is the same split the I/O surface draws
152	as the fast-path ack vs the durable sink — see
153	[`commands-io.md`](./commands-io.md).)
154	
155	### Batching (design decision)
156	Both channels batch:
157	
158	- **Disk writes are batched.** The journaler fsyncs a *range* of sequence numbers
159	  per syscall rather than one-per-command (group commit). One fsync durably
160	  commits the whole batch and advances the durable cursor in a single step.
161	- **Acks ride the durable cursor.** When an fsync commits a range, every command
162	  in it is acked as *sequenced* in one batch.
163	- **Outbox writes are batched.** Post-fold outcome events are appended to the
164	  outbox in batches behind the durable cursor.
165	
166	This amortizes syscall cost over many commands. Throughput rises with batch size
167	while latency stays bounded, because the buffer naturally forms batches
168	proportional to load — heavier load, bigger batches, same wall-clock.
169	
170	A batch flushes when it **fills *or* when a small max-delay elapses**, whichever
171	comes first — start at **~100 µs** and tune against the p99.99 budget. Under load
172	batches fill before the timer; under light load the timer bounds added latency,
173	so a lone command never parks behind an empty pipeline.
174	
175	### Numeric & iteration determinism — the bake-off's pass/fail line
176	Determinism below the sequencer is what makes two implementations replay-equivalent,
177	and the **numeric rules are where correct-looking implementations diverge first** —
178	each language spells integer overflow, division, and map iteration differently, so
179	anything left to the language silently diverges, often only on the first funding tick.
180	These rules are therefore a **spec mandate, not a per-implementation choice**, and each
181	is **mechanically enforced** (below), never left to review. They are the contract two
182	implementations must meet to replay a log bit-for-bit.
183	
184	- **Scaled integers at a declared, per-field scale.** Each value is a scaled integer
185	  at a fixed per-field scale: price in integer **ticks**, size in integer **lots**,
186	  USD and collateral amounts as **1e8-scaled** integers, rates/ratios at their own
187	  fixed scale (`commands-io.md` §Conventions). No decimal type and no floating
188	  point anywhere in business logic.
189	- **`uint128` intermediates.** Intermediate products (notional = price × size, the
190	  funding pot, PnL) are taken in a **wider integer — `uint128`** — and only then
191	  rescaled, so the rescale never loses bits a 64-bit product would. If even the
192	  128-bit intermediate would overflow, that is a **Typed Overflow Reject** (below),
193	  not a wrap. Go has no native 128-bit integer — an implementation uses `math/bits` or
194	  a two-limb struct — but the *result* is bit-identical to the languages that do.
195	- **Typed Overflow Reject.** Integer overflow is **rejected with a typed reason**
196	  (`ARITHMETIC_OVERFLOW`), never wrapped, never saturated, and never allowed to leave
197	  partial state — a rejected command's effects are atomic, so state is left exactly as
198	  before. This must be a spec mandate because the **default differs per language**: Go
199	  and Java wrap silently, Rust wraps in release builds, Zig traps. The check is on
200	  **combined totals** (e.g. used + reserved margin), not just isolated fields — the
201	  overflow that bites is usually a sum, not a single product.
202	- **Per-Operation Rounding.** Every operation that rescales or divides **names its
203	  rounding mode and its negative/modulo behaviour**. This is the rule with no analogue
204	  in an integer-ledger system that never divides, and the first thing to diverge on a
205	  funding tick if left unstated. The named modes are **floor** (toward −∞),
206	  **truncate** (toward zero — the native `/` of Go, Rust, Zig, and Java; its modulo
207	  takes the sign of the dividend), and **half-even** (banker's: nearest, ties to even).
208	  Each money operation pins one; the per-operation assignments live with the formulas
209	  in the behaviour spec (`behaviour-spec.md`). **Any division not otherwise named
210	  defaults to truncate (toward zero)**, so a bare `/` is well-defined; a division that
211	  needs another mode must say so. The major assignments:
212	
213	  | Operation | Intermediate | Rounding |
214	  |---|---|---|
215	  | Notional for margin-tier lookup (`abs(qty) × price`) | `uint128` | half-even, to integer |
216	  | Margin requirements (IMR / MMR / RMR) and fees | `uint128` | half-even, 6 dp |
217	  | Raw & adjusted collateral value | `uint128` | half-even, 6 dp |
218	  | Funding payment (`qty × index × rate`) | `uint128` | truncate (toward zero) |
219	  | Bankruptcy & liquidation price | `uint128` | truncate (toward zero) |
220	  | Collateral-exchange collateral taken | `uint128` | half-even, to asset precision |
221	  | Collateral-exchange USDT credited | `uint128` | floor (account's favour) |
222	  | Non-USDT withdrawable cap | `uint128` | truncate, to asset precision |
223	  | Any other rescale / division | `uint128` | truncate (the default) |
224	
225	- **Fixed-width integers only.** Domain logic uses fixed-width types (`int64`,
226	  `uint64`, `uint128`, …) exclusively. Architecture-dependent widths — `usize`/`isize`,
227	  Go `int`/`uint`, C `int`/`long` — are **banned below the sequencer**: their width
228	  changes with the host, which breaks replay across hardware.
229	- **Deterministic iteration — no hash-map iteration in the fold.** Any reduction over a
230	  collection below the sequencer iterates in a **defined order keyed on an explicit
231	  field**. Language hash-map iteration is **banned in the fold**: Go deliberately
232	  randomizes map order, Rust seeds its hasher per process, Java's order depends on
233	  capacity — so a fold over a hash map diverges even between two runs of *one* correct
234	  build. State that is iterated uses arrays, sorted structures, or an index with an
235	  explicit key.
236	- **Total-Order Tiebreak on merge.** When parallel work is merged back (the detect→apply
237	  fan-in below), the merge imposes a **total order** — no tie left to worker-arrival
238	  order. The comparator is **byte-identical across languages** and carries **secondary
239	  keys** wherever the primary key (e.g. account id) can tie.
240	- **Mechanical enforcement.** Each ban is enforced by the toolchain, not by review — a
241	  review-only ban eventually leaks one float into a rate or PnL calc: Zig `comptime`
242	  reject of float types in the domain modules; Rust `clippy` deny on float arithmetic
243	  plus a module boundary that exports no float types; Go `go vet` / a CI grep for
244	  `float`/`math/big` in the domain packages; Java an ArchUnit rule forbidding
245	  `float`/`double` and `BigDecimal` in the fold.
246	- **Verification.** All of the above is checked by **replay-equivalence tests**: replay
247	  the same log on two builds (ideally two languages) and assert bit-identical state and
248	  snapshots.
249	
250	### Cross-account operations & stop-the-world
251	Most commands touch one account and are cheap. But some com
  119. assistantassistant2026-06-27 21:03:13 UTC
    thinking
  120. assistantassistant2026-06-27 21:03:28 UTC
    Yes — I've now read `system-design.md` cover to cover myself (532 lines), not just via the agents. And reading it changes the framing of my answer in a useful way, so I'm glad you pushed.

**The key realization: that document *is* the target architecture, and my recommendation is largely "converge rust-raw back to its own spec."** Almost every item the three agents proposed is already mandated there — they independently re-derived the design that's already written down. Specifically:

- **Durable input log as the replay source** — §1, §2 (principle 3 "the log is the source of truth"), §3 "The sequence log… each entry is the fully-resolved input command… what a recovering process replays." This is exactly the reframe I gave you (journal *inputs*, not the `.obox`), stated as spec. rust-raw persists this log *nowhere* today.
- **The three-consumer ring — Journaler / Matching / Publisher on separate cursors** — §4, with the ack (post-journal "sequenced", on the durable cursor) kept distinct from outcome events (post-fold, to the outbox, behind `min(matching, durable)`). rust-raw **collapsed all three into one fold thread** (`sequencer.rs:308`) and pushes the ack *before* anything is durable. That collapse is the single biggest divergence.
- **Zero-copy ingress→log, "bytes written once in the form they arrived"** — §2 principle 6 ("one binary format end to end… avoids write amplification"). This is a *spec mandate*, not an optimization I'm inventing — it directly justifies the Read-Into-Slot ring and Encode-In-Place outbox.
- **Group-commit fsync, ~100µs batch window** — §4 and §8 ("group-commit batching to amortize fsync"). Spec-prescribed, absent in rust-raw.
- **Two fsync'd logs (input log + outbox), exactly-once outbox keyed on sequence number, publisher tracks last-written seq durably** — §8.
- **Snapshots + recovery-time model, dedup in journaled state** — §5, §7, §9.

**One thing the doc sharpens that the agents (and your brief) understated:** the latency target is **p99.99 ≈ 5 ms end-to-end** (§6), while the ~1µs is the *compute spine*. That matters for the fsync tension: the agents framed it as "fsync vs 1µs," but the real ack-latency budget is millisecond-scale. A ~100µs group-commit window fits inside a 5ms end-to-end budget with two orders of magnitude to spare — so commit-before-ack (the strict, correct posture) is comfortably affordable, and the "tension" is even softer than I described. The 1µs constraint only governs the *fold*, which stays syscall-free.

So my synthesis stands, but the right way to pitch it to you is: **rust-raw is a heavily-optimized compute core that has drifted off the system-design contract on the I/O/durability axes — the work is re-establishing the spec's journaler/durable-cursor/zero-copy seams, not inventing new architecture.** The `frozen-obox` goldens + the determinism property make that convergence verifiable byte-for-byte.

Want me to write this up as a **gap analysis — "system-design.md vs rust-raw as-built"** (a table of each invariant in §10's checklist mapped to its current rust-raw status + the closing change), and drop it under `experiments/single-sequencer/docs/`? That's more actionable than the generic roadmap, since it anchors every recommendation to a contract line you already own.

  130. useruser2026-06-27 21:19:13 UTC
    tool result
    # Glossary — single-sequencer spec
## The message path
- **Request** (`*Request`) — a sequenced input: any record appended to the durable log in
  so it carries no outcome — only the assigned sequence number and a disposition
  (`SEQUENCED` or `SHED`). It is **one shared type for every Request**, never per-command,
  because at ack time the fold has not run. It says *sequenced*, never *filled*.
- **Response** (`*Response`) — a post-fold record written to the durable sink: the outcome
- Every sequenced input is `<Subject>Request` — the subject noun plus `Request`; the
- The non-sequenced **read path** is separate and named `<Subject>Query` → `<Subject>Result`
## Core engine
- **Single global sequencer** — the one writer that stamps every Request with a monotonic
  **sequence number (`Seq`)**, fixing the total order. Below it, state is a pure function of
- **Ring buffer & consumers** — the pre-allocated buffer the sequencer publishes into, read
  in parallel by three consumers: the **Journaler** (append + `fsync`, advance the durable
  cursor), the **Matching** consumer (apply to in-memory books/state), the **Publisher**
  (write Responses to the outbox).
- **Sink / Outbox** — the local, `fsync`'d log of Responses, the same container format as
  the input journal. An external relay drains it; the core's job ends at "written to the
  outbox". Keyed on `Seq` for exactly-once.
  is no separate client id — the client *is* the subaccount. The sequencer replays the original
- **`MeSeq`** — the matching-engine fill sequence, the causal order of fills within a match.
- **Snapshot / replay / recovery** — recovery is "load latest snapshot at `S`, then replay
  the journal tail from `S+1`". Snapshots are derived data, always re-derivable from the log.
## Prices & time
  last is the last traded price. All arrive in a sequenced `PriceBatchRequest`.
## Margin & risk
## Liquidation & backstops
## Orders
  refreshed (a `ScheduleCancelRequest`).
## Numeric determinism
=== existing docs ===
behaviour-spec.md
behaviour-test-plan.md
commands-io.md
commands-wire.md
GLOSSARY.md
system-design.md
tigerbeetle-harvest.md

    tool: Write
    {
  "file_path": "/home/grace/.herdr/worktrees/synfinex/single-sequencer-consolidated/experiments/single-sequencer/docs/rust-raw-spec-gap-and-roadmap.md",
  "content": "# rust-raw — spec gap analysis and convergence roadmap\n\nHow the `rust-raw` implementation (`impl/rust-raw`, crate `spine`) stands against the\n[system-design](./system-design.md) contract, and the order in which to close the gap.\n\nThe short version: `rust-raw` is a heavily-optimised **compute core** that has drifted off\nthe contract on the **I/O, journaling, and durability** seams. The matching fold already\nhonours the determinism and numeric invariants. What is missing is the durable input\njournal, the three-consumer ring with a real durable cursor, group commit, replay/recovery,\nsnapshots, and zero-copy across the transport. The work is *re-establishing seams the spec\nalready specifies*, not inventing new architecture.\n\n---\n\n## As-built status against the Design Invariants checklist\n\nGrouped by the cluster each invariant belongs to. \"Met\" = the implementation satisfies the\ncontract line today; \"Partial\" = the compute core satisfies it but the pipeline around it\ndoes not; \"Gap\" = absent.\n\n### Compute core and determinism — essentially met\n\n| Invariant | Status | Evidence |\n|---|---|---|\n| Exactly one writer assigns `Seq` | Met | single core thread owns `next_seq` |\n| One global sequencer, no per-market sharding | Met | one `Sequencer` over all markets |\n| No wall-clock / RNG / non-deterministic iteration in the fold | Met | `self.now` set only from `ClockTickRequest.now` (`sequencer.rs:1008`); `now_mono_ns()` only in telemetry outside the fold (`sequencer.rs:310`); client-order-id map is lookup-only, never iterated for emission |\n| Time enters only as sequenced clock ticks | Met | `ClockTickRequest` path |\n| Scaled-integer money math, `uint128` intermediates, no float | Met | `#![deny(clippy::float_arithmetic)]` (`lib.rs:1`), `num.rs` |\n| Integer overflow is a typed reject | Met | `ARITHMETIC_OVERFLOW` reject path in the fold |\n| Every rescale/division names its rounding mode | Met | rounding pinned in `num.rs` / `matcher.rs` |\n| Deterministic iteration — no hash-map iteration in the fold | Met | iterated state is array/slab/indexed |\n| Float/decimal ban enforced mechanically | Met | clippy deny + float-free module boundary |\n| Dedup table in journaled state, deterministic | Met | dedup map + arenas in `Sequencer` state (`sequencer.rs:122-130`) |\n| Inputs from durable queues deduped by upstream id (deposits) | Met | source-identity dedup in journaled state |\n| Clients carry idempotency keys; retries deduped | Met | `Idem`, dedup collapse to original outcome |\n| Every record carries a `(version, type)` header | Met (wire) | `SCHEMA_VERSION` + `template_id` (`wire/mod.rs`) |\n\n### Transport and zero-copy — partial; the first work cluster\n\n| Invariant | Status | Evidence / closing change |\n|---|---|---|\n| Hot path allocation-free in steady state | Partial | the **fold** is alloc-free, but the **pipeline** allocates per message: ring push copies into the slot (`ring.rs:101`) and pop `to_vec`s out (`ring.rs:137`); `net.rs` allocates twice per frame (`net.rs:308,314`); `encode_ack_record` allocates (`sequencer.rs:332`); the outbox allocates a fresh `Vec` per append (`outbox.rs:27`) |\n| Same binary format wire → log → replay, no re-encode, no write amplification | Gap | there is **no input log at all**, so the mandated zero-copy ingress→log does not exist; ingress bytes are decoded and dropped (`net.rs:220`, `sequencer.rs:307`) |\n| Backpressure sheds explicitly at the boundary (capacity and age) | Partial | malformed shed exists; ring-full **spins/blocks** rather than shedding, and age-based (Δt) shed is absent |\n\n### Journaling, durability, and recovery — the dominant gap; the second work cluster\n\n| Invariant | Status | Evidence / closing change |\n|---|---|---|\n| Nothing user-visible emitted before the command is durably logged | Gap | the `SEQUENCED` ack is pushed **before** anything is durable (`sequencer.rs:313`); the outbox is `flush()`'d only at shutdown (`sequencer.rs:326`) and **never `fsync`'d** |\n| Durability is single-node local `fsync` | Gap | no `fsync`/`fdatasync` anywhere |\n| Disk writes, acks, outbox writes batched (group commit, ~100µs) | Gap | no batching; per-message acks; per-frame outbox writes |\n| Ack (post-journal, on the durable cursor) and outcome events (post-fold) kept distinct on separate cursors | Gap | the **Journaler, Matching, and Publisher are collapsed into one thread** (`sequencer.rs:308`); there is one cursor and no durable cursor |\n| Outputs to a local `fsync`'d outbox on the same substrate | Partial | an outbox file exists and the relay drains it, but it is not `fsync`'d |\n| Outbox writes exactly-once (keyed on `Seq`, last-written cursor persisted) | Gap | `Outbox::create` uses `File::create`, which **truncates on restart** (`outbox.rs:16`); no persisted last-written cursor; no append-on-restart |\n| State reproducible from snapshot + log replay, bit-for-bit | Gap | no replay path; no snapshot |\n| Snapshots never block the sequencer, always log-recoverable | Gap | no snapshot mechanism |\n| Snapshot interval set from a restart-time target | Gap | no snapshot |\n| Metrics derived off-thread from ring-buffer cursors | Partial | telemetry stamps ingress/publish timestamps, but cursors are not exposed and sampling is not on a separate thread |\n\n### Future performance levers — out of current scope, tracked\n\n| Invariant | Status | Note |\n|---|---|---|\n| Cross-account fan-out finishes within budget; max fan-out cost tracked | Partial | the fold is deterministic (single-threaded) but fan-out is **not parallelised** and max fan-out cost is not a first-class signal — a later throughput lever |\n| Parallel results merge under a total order with secondary keys | Not yet | no parallel fan-out exists to merge yet |\n| Behaviour changes via snapshot-as-new-genesis | Gap | depends on the snapshot mechanism (second cluster) |\n| Withdrawals write an idempotent intent to the outbox | Per behaviour scope | governed by `behaviour-spec.md`, not this seam work |\n\n**The gap collapses to two clusters that map one-to-one onto the stated priorities:**\nthe **zero-copy transport + ring topology** cluster, and the **durable journal + replay**\ncluster.\n\n---\n\n## Why re-architect before adding durability\n\nThe two clusters must land in this order, and the reason is measurement honesty, not\nconvenience.\n\nThe current latency gate (`benches/fast_path.rs`) drives `accept_with_sink` against an\nin-memory sink — it never touches the ring, the socket, the ack path, or the disk. So it\nmeasures **compute only**. That cuts two ways: transport zero-copy work will not move the\ngate, and `fsync` will not regress it. If both clusters land together, a regression in one\nis indistinguishable from a win in the other, and the like-for-like comparison the bake-off\ndepends on is lost.\n\nSplitting the work fixes this:\n\n- The **re-architecture phase changes structure and ownership but not durability and not\n  emitted bytes.** It installs the three-consumer ring, the separate cursors, and the\n  zero-copy buffer ownership — with a **staging-only Journaler that does no `fsync`** and a\n  durable cursor that advances immediately. Because no byte of output changes and no `fsync`\n  is paid, the frozen `frozen-obox` goldens prove behavioural equivalence and the existing\n  compute gate proves no compute regression, in isolation.\n- The **durability phase then drops real behaviour into seams that already exist.** Its cost\n  surfaces only in a *new* durable-cursor / ack-latency measurement, never confounding the\n  compute number.\n\nThe two axes are never measured together, which is exactly what keeps the comparison honest.\n\n---\n\n## Phase one — compute-preserving re-architecture (no durability)\n\nGoal: the spec's ring topology and zero-copy ownership, with durability stubbed, proven to\nnot regress compute.\n\n- **Buffer-Ownership Ring.** Replace the copy-in / `to_vec`-out SPSC ring with ownership\n  transfer of pre-allocated slots. A fill-in-place producer (`reserve` → `read_exact` lands\n  the frame body directly in the slot → `commit(len)`) collapses the two `net.rs` ingress\n  allocations and the producer copy into a single kernel→slot copy. A borrowing consumer (a\n  pop guard that lends `&[u8]` and advances the head on drop) removes the pop allocation. The\n  fold already takes `&[u8]` (`sequencer.rs:173`), so it composes unchanged. The\n  single-producer/single-consumer split is what makes the borrow sound: the producer cannot\n  touch a slot until the consumer advances the head.\n- **Three-consumer ring with a staging-only Journaler.** Split the single core thread into\n  the **Journaler**, **Matching**, and **Publisher** consumers on separate cursors, exactly\n  as the contract draws them. In this phase the Journaler is memory-only — it stages input\n  bytes and advances the **durable cursor immediately, with no `fsync`**. The Publisher gates\n  outbox writes on `min(matching, durable)`, and the `SEQUENCED` ack rides the durable\n  cursor. This installs the entire commit-before-ack *seam* without paying for durability, so\n  the durability phase becomes a localised change behind the cursor rather than a re-thread.\n- **Encode-In-Place outbox.** Encode Responses directly into a reusable buffer / staging\n  region instead of a fresh `Vec` per append; `encode_log_frame_record` already takes a\n  caller buffer (`wire/mod.rs:415`). Removes the per-frame outbox allocation.\n- **Batched vectored egress.** Set `TCP_NODELAY` once; drain ready acks and emit them with a\n  single vectored write per wakeup instead of `write_all` + `flush` per message\n  (`net.rs:257-261`). The only latency-visible change in this phase.\n\n**Phase-one gates (must all hold before phase two):**\n- `frozen-obox` goldens byte-identical (behaviour unchanged).\n- `cargo test --lib`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` green; ring tests extended\n  to the `reserve`/`commit` and pop-guard APIs (ideally a loom/miri check on slot borrows\n  under wraparound).\n- Compute gate (`fast_path`, rdtscp p50/p99/p99.9) unchanged or better.\n- A new **end-to-end pipeline allocation gate** using `alloc_counter` (`src/alloc_counter.rs`)\n  asserting zero steady-state allocations across net → ring → fold → outbox — because the\n  compute gate cannot see this path.\n\n---\n\n## Phase two — durability on the installed seam\n\nGoal: real durability and replay, dropped into the cursors and buffers phase one already\nprovides.\n\n- **Sequenced Command Journal.** The Journaler appends the raw input bytes (zero-copy from\n  the owned slot — \"bytes written once in the form they arrived\") to an append-only file and\n  `fdatasync`s a **range per group commit** (~100µs window or batch-fill). The durable cursor\n  now advances on `fsync`, not immediately — so the ack becomes genuinely commit-before-ack\n  with **no change to the ack path**, because the cursor seam already exists. Add the\n  per-frame parent hash-chain to the existing CRC frame (`wire/mod.rs:411-432`) so the log is\n  self-verifying and the chain head at `Seq` N is a single equivalence witness.\n- **Replay and recovery.** On startup, walk frames with `validate_log_frame`, verify the\n  chain, and replay from `snapshot.Seq + 1` through the **unmodified** `accept_with_sink`,\n  which rebuilds books, accounts, id generators, and the dedup arenas for free (dedup already\n  lives in journaled state). Recovery policy in three cases: clean; **torn tail** at/after the\n  durable cursor → truncate to the last chain-consistent frame; **interior corruption** inside\n  the `fsync`'d region → halt and declare loss, never silently truncate. Verification is free:\n  replay must regenerate the golden `.obox` byte-for-byte (the determinism property makes the\n  frozen goldens the recovery oracle).\n- **Exactly-once outbox.** Stop truncating on restart (`File::create` at `outbox.rs:16`); key\n  the outbox on `Seq`, persist the last-written cursor, and write only past the durable\n  cursor, so replay resumes where it left off and a re-derived record with a known `Seq` is\n  dropped.\n- **Snapshots and truncation.** Periodic off-thread snapshot via `Sequencer::clone()`\n  (already `Clone`, `sequencer.rs:132`), written fresh → `fdatasync` → checksum-verify →\n  atomic manifest flip → only then truncate the journal behind the snapshot. Sets the\n  restart-time knob from a stated target.\n- **I/O hardening (after the simple path is correct).** `O_DIRECT` + FUA on the journal,\n  `fsync(dir_fd)` after segment creation, `fsync(fd)` on startup, and `io_uring` batching the\n  socket and disk submissions per tick once the segment-ownership model is in place.\n\n**Phase-two gates:**\n- The phase-one compute gate and allocation gate stay green (durability must not touch the\n  hot path's compute or allocation profile).\n- A **separate durable-cursor / ack-latency benchmark** — the compute gate cannot see\n  `fsync`, so durability needs its own number; report *sequenced* latency and *durably-acked*\n  latency distinctly.\n- **Crash-injection in the `fsync` window** proving no acked command is ever lost and no\n  un-acked command is ever durable.\n- **Replay-equivalence and torn-tail tests** against the goldens; the reject-then-continue\n  and deposit-redelivery goldens are the load-bearing fixtures because they exercise rebuilt\n  dedup and restored state.\n- Recovery-time numbers fit the stated restart-time target.\n\n---\n\n## The fsync-versus-latency question, settled\n\nThe end-to-end latency target is **p99.99 ≈ 5 ms** ([system-design §6](./system-design.md));\nthe ~1µs figure governs the **compute fold only**. A ~100µs group-commit window therefore\nfits inside the end-to-end budget with two orders of magnitude to spare, and the fold never\ncalls `fsync` (it rides a different thread and a different cursor). Commit-before-ack — the\nstrict, correct posture — is affordable. The apparent conflict only exists while the\nJournaler is collapsed into the fold thread, which phase one removes.\n"
}
